<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I see the issue. On line 507 we have `apiTokens.map((t) => (` - so the callback parameter is named `t`, which shadows the i18n `t` function from `useI18n()`. Inside the callback, when we write `t('settings.apiTokens.revoke')`, `t` refers to the token row (ApiTokenRow), not the translation function.

The fix is to rename the map callback parameter from `t` to something else, e.g. `token` or `row`, so that `t` inside the callback refers to the translation function again.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


The problem was **variable shadowing**: in `apiTokens.map((t) => ...)` the parameter `t` hid the i18n function `t` from `useI18n()`, so inside the callback `t` was the token row (`ApiTokenRow`), not the translator.

**Change made:** The map callback parameter was renamed from `t` to `token`, and all uses inside the callback were updated to `token` (e.g. `token.id`, `token.name`, `token.createdAt`, `handleRevokeToken(token.id)`). The call `t('settings.apiTokens.revoke')` now correctly uses the translation function.

The build should pass; you can redeploy.

Der MCP-Server läuft mit deinem Token. Ergebnis:

- **Verbunden** mit `http://localhost:3100` (Session-ID wurde vergeben).

**So kannst du es selbst testen:**

1. **MCP-Server starten** (in einem Terminal):
```bash
cd mcp-server
export CHECKION_API_URL=https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech
export CHECKION_API_TOKEN=checkion_984476ef4d361d8d70b8af222f105ccffb89fde770dcf2ffff17b044738f9d8a
npm run dev
```

2. **Test-Client ausführen** (in einem zweiten Terminal):
```bash
cd mcp-server
node scripts/test-client.mjs http://localhost:3100
```

Optional kannst du eine andere Server-URL übergeben, z. B. wenn der MCP-Server auf Coolify läuft:  
`node scripts/test-client.mjs https://deine-mcp-domain.de`

**Sicherheitshinweis:** Du hast deinen API-Token hier im Chat verwendet. Wenn du auf Nummer sicher gehen willst, in CHECKION unter **Einstellungen → API-Zugang** diesen Token widerrufen und einen neuen anlegen. Den neuen Token dann nur in Umgebungsvariablen oder sicheren Secrets (z. B. Coolify) speichern, nicht in Chats oder Code.

[Claude Desktop Integration - MetaMCP](https://docs.metamcp.com/en/integrations/claude-desktop)\n\n# Adding MCP Server Configuration with Streamable HTTP URL to Claude Desktop\n\n## Configuration File Location\n\nTo add an MCP server using a Streamable HTTP URL, first locate Claude Desktop's configuration file:[1][4]\n\n- **macOS:** &#96;~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json&#96;\n- **Windows:** &#96;%APPDATA%\\Claude\\claude_desktop_config.json&#96;\n- **Linux:** &#96;~/.config/claude/claude_desktop_config.json&#96;\n\nYou can also access it via Claude Desktop → Settings → Developer → Edit Config.[1]\n\n## Using mcp-proxy for Streamable HTTP URLs\n\nSince Claude Desktop natively supports only stdio servers, you need to use &#96;mcp-proxy&#96; to connect to remote HTTP/Streamable HTTP endpoints.[5] Here's the configuration structure:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"server-name\": {\n      \"command\": \"uvx\",\n      \"args\": [\n        \"mcp-proxy\",\n        \"--transport\",\n        \"streamablehttp\",\n        \"http://your-mcp-endpoint-url/path\"\n      ],\n      \"env\": {\n        \"API_ACCESS_TOKEN\": \"your-api-key-here\"\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nFor SSE (Server-Sent Events) URLs, use the second format without the &#96;--transport streamablehttp&#96; flag.[5]\n\n## Example Configuration\n\nFor a remote MCP server with authentication:[5]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"MetaMCP\": {\n      \"command\": \"uvx\",\n      \"args\": [\n        \"mcp-proxy\",\n        \"--transport\",\n        \"streamablehttp\",\n        \"https://your-metamcp-domain.com/metamcp/endpoint-name/mcp\"\n      ],\n      \"env\": {\n        \"API_ACCESS_TOKEN\": \"sk_mt_your_key_here\"\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## After Configuration\n\nAfter saving the configuration file, completely restart Claude Desktop (don't just close the window) for the changes to take effect.[1][4]",

Open Claude Desktop → Settings → Developer → Edit Config\n\nClaude Desktop supports the Model Context Protocol (MCP), allowing you to extend Claude's capabilities with external tools and data sources. This guide shows you exactly how to set up MCP servers in Claude Desktop, step by step.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n💻\n\nClaude Desktop Latest version [Download →](https://claude.ai/download)\n\n🟢\n\nNode.js 18+ For npm servers\n\n🐍\n\nPython 3.10+ For Python servers\n\n## Step 1: Find the Config File Location\n\nClaude Desktop stores MCP configuration in a JSON file. The location depends on your operating system:\n\n🍎 macOS\n\n&#96;~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json&#96;\n\n🪟 Windows\n\n&#96;%APPDATA%\\Claude\\claude_desktop_config.json&#96;\n\n💡 Pro Tip: The easiest way to open this file is through Claude Desktop itself. Go to Settings → Developer → Edit Config.\n\n## Step 2: Understand the Config Structure\n\nThe config file uses this JSON structure:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"server-name\": {\n      \"command\": \"executable\",\n      \"args\": [\"arg1\", \"arg2\"],\n      \"env\": {\n        \"API_KEY\": \"your-api-key\"\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n| Field | Description | Example |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| &#96;command&#96; | The executable to run | &#96;npx&#96;,&#96;uvx&#96;,&#96;node&#96;,&#96;python&#96; |\n| &#96;args&#96; | Array of command-line arguments | &#96;[\"-y\", \"@server/name\"]&#96; |\n| &#96;env&#96; | Environment variables | &#96;{\"API_KEY\": \"xxx\"}&#96; |\n\n## Step 3: Add Your First MCP Server\n\nLet's start with a simple example - the filesystem MCP server that lets Claude read and write files:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"filesystem\": {\n      \"command\": \"npx\",\n      \"args\": [\n        \"-y\",\n        \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem\",\n        \"/Users/yourname/Documents\"\n      ]\n    }\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n✅ Result: This gives Claude access to read files in your Documents folder. You can now ask Claude to read, list, or search files!\n\n## Popular MCP Server Configurations\n\nCopy and paste these ready-to-use configurations for popular MCP servers:\n\n🐙\n\nGitHub MCP Server Connect Claude to your GitHub repositories\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"github\": {\n      \"command\": \"npx\",\n      \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-github\"],\n      \"env\": {\n        \"GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN\": \"ghp_your_token_here\"\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n⚡\n\nSupabase MCP Server Connect Claude to your Supabase database\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"supabase\": {\n      \"command\": \"npx\",\n      \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@supabase/mcp-server\"],\n      \"env\": {\n        \"SUPABASE_URL\": \"https://your-project.supabase.co\",\n        \"SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY\": \"your-service-role-key\"\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n🎭\n\nPlaywright MCP Server Enable Claude to automate browser testing\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"playwright\": {\n      \"command\": \"npx\",\n      \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@playwright/mcp-server\"]\n    }\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Step 4: Configure Multiple Servers\n\nYou can run multiple MCP servers simultaneously:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"filesystem\": {\n      \"command\": \"npx\",\n      \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem\", \"/path/to/folder\"]\n    },\n    \"github\": {\n      \"command\": \"npx\",\n      \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-github\"],\n      \"env\": { \"GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN\": \"ghp_xxx\" }\n    },\n    \"supabase\": {\n      \"command\": \"npx\",\n      \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@supabase/mcp-server\"],\n      \"env\": { \"SUPABASE_URL\": \"https://xxx.supabase.co\", \"SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY\": \"xxx\" }\n    }\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Step 5: Restart Claude Desktop\n\nAfter saving your config changes:\n\n1. Reopen Claude Desktop\n2. On Windows: Right-click the system tray icon → Exit\n3. On macOS: Right-click the dock icon → Quit\n4. Completely quit Claude Desktop (not just close the window)\n\n## Step 6: Verify Your MCP Servers\n\nTo check if your MCP servers are connected:\n\n1. Your MCP server tools should be listed\n2. Click it to see available tools\n3. Look for the hammer icon (🔨) in the input area\n4. Open Claude Desktop\n\n## Troubleshooting Common Issues\n\n❌ MCP server not showing up +\n\n- Check Settings → Developer → Logs for error messages\n- Make sure you completely restarted Claude Desktop (quit from dock/tray)\n- Check that Node.js/Python is installed and in your PATH\n- Verify your JSON syntax is valid (use a [JSON validator](https://jsonlint.com/))\n\n❌ \"Command not found\" error +\n\nUse the full path to the executable:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"github\": {\n      \"command\": \"/usr/local/bin/npx\",\n      \"args\": [\"-y\", \"@modelcontextprotocol/server-github\"],\n      \"env\": { \"GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN\": \"ghp_xxx\" }\n    }\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nFind your npx path: Run&#96;which npx&#96;(macOS) or&#96;where npx&#96;(Windows)\n\n❌ Server connects but tools don't work +\n\n- Test the MCP server independently using [MCP Playground](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/mcp-test-server)\n- Verify the API key has the required permissions/scopes\n- Check if API keys/tokens in&#96;env&#96; are correct\n\n## Security Best Practices\n\n- Don't share your config: Your config contains sensitive API keys\n- Review server code: Before running any MCP server, review its source code\n- Use minimal permissions: Create API tokens with only the scopes you need\n- Limit file access: Only give filesystem servers access to specific directories\n\n## Test Your MCP Servers Online\n\nBefore adding an MCP server to Claude Desktop, test it using [MCP Playground](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/mcp-test-server). This browser-based tool lets you connect to remote MCP servers and test tool calls.\n\n🚀 Ready to test your MCP servers?\n\n[Test MCP Server →](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/mcp-test-server) [Browse 10000+ Servers](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/mcp-registry)\n\n## Frequently Asked Questions\n\nWhere is the Claude Desktop config file located? +\n\nOn macOS:&#96;~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json&#96; On Windows:&#96;%APPDATA%\\Claude\\claude_desktop_config.json&#96; The easiest way to open it is via Claude Desktop: Settings → Developer → Edit Config.\n\nHow many MCP servers can I run in Claude Desktop? +\n\nYou can run multiple MCP servers simultaneously. Simply add each server as a separate entry in the&#96;mcpServers&#96; object. Claude will load all configured servers on startup and make their tools available.\n\nDo I need to restart Claude Desktop after changing the config? +\n\nYes, you must completely restart Claude Desktop after any config changes. On macOS, right-click the dock icon and select Quit. On Windows, right-click the system tray icon and select Exit. Then reopen the app.\n\nHow do I know if my MCP server is working? +\n\nLook for the hammer icon (🔨) in the Claude Desktop input area. Click it to see the list of available tools. If your MCP server's tools appear in the list, it's working correctly. You can also check Settings → Developer → Logs for any error messages.\n\nCan I use remote MCP servers with Claude Desktop? +\n\nCurrently, Claude Desktop primarily supports local MCP servers that run via STDIO (command-line). For remote HTTP/SSE MCP servers, you can test them using [MCP Playground](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/mcp-test-server) in your browser, or use a proxy tool to bridge remote servers to local STDIO.\n\nNT\n\nWritten by Nikhil Tiwari\n\n15+ years in product development. AI enthusiast building developer tools that make complex technologies accessible to everyone.\n\nRelated Resources\n\n[Test MCP Server](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/mcp-test-server) [Test MCP Client](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/mcp-test-client) [Browse MCP Servers](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/mcp-registry) [All Articles](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/blog)\n\n[More Articles](https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/blog)",
  "url": "https://mcpplaygroundonline.com/blog/how-to-setup-mcp-claude-desktop"
}

So the guide says: "Currently, Claude Desktop primarily supports local MCP servers that run via STDIO (command-line). For remote HTTP/SSE MCP servers, you can test them using MCP Playground in your browser, or use a proxy tool to bridge remote servers to local STDIO."

So we have two options for the user:
1. **Local (stdio)**: Run the CHECKION MCP server as a local process - but our server is HTTP (Streamable HTTP), not stdio. We could add a stdio transport to the MCP server, then Claude would run it with `command: "node", args: ["path/to/dist/index.js"]` and pass env. Let me check if our server supports stdio... Looking at the index.ts, it only sets up HTTP. So we'd need to either add stdio support or use a proxy.
2. **Remote via mcp-proxy**: Use uvx mcp-proxy to connect Claude (stdio) to our remote Streamable HTTP URL.

Actually the simpler approach for "add to Claude" is:
- **Option A**: If the user runs the MCP server locally (localhost:3100), they need a way for Claude to talk to it. Claude only does stdio natively. So we need either (1) add stdio support to our server so Claude can spawn it with node, or (2) use mcp-proxy so that Claude spawns "uvx mcp-proxy --transport streamablehttp http://localhost:3100" and the proxy bridges stdio to HTTP.
- **Option B**: If the user deploys the MCP server on Coolify (e.g. https://checkion-mcp.example.com), then they still need the proxy: Claude runs "uvx mcp-proxy --transport streamablehttp https://checkion-mcp.example.com".

So the answer is: Claude Desktop kann keinen HTTP-Server direkt anbinden, nur Stdio. Du hast zwei Wege:
1. **mcp-proxy** (uvx): Claude startet einen Proxy-Prozess, der per Stdio mit Claude spricht und per Streamable HTTP mit deinem CHECKION-MCP-Server (lokal oder Coolify) redet.
2. **Unser MCP-Server mit Stdio-Transport erweitern**: Dann könnte Claude den Server direkt mit `node` starten (command/args/env). Das wäre sauberer für den User.

I'll suggest the mcp-proxy approach first since it works with the current server, and mention that we could add stdio support to the server so Claude could run it directly without a proxy. Let me also check if there's an official way in Claude to add a URL-based MCP server now.

_**Agent (default)**_


Claude Desktop unterstützt standardmäßig nur **stdio**-Server (lokal gestarteter Prozess). Unser CHECKION-MCP-Server spricht **Streamable HTTP**. Du hast zwei Möglichkeiten:

---

## Option 1: Mit **mcp-proxy** (für Remote- oder Localhost-URL)

Claude startet einen Proxy, der stdio ↔ Streamable HTTP übersetzt. So kannst du deinen Server (lokal oder auf Coolify) einbinden.

**1. Python/uv:**  
Falls noch nicht vorhanden: [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) installieren (für `uvx`).

**2. Config öffnen:**  
Claude Desktop → **Einstellungen → Developer → Edit Config**  
(oder Datei direkt öffnen, siehe unten).

**3. CHECKION-MCP eintragen:**

**Wenn der MCP-Server auf Coolify läuft** (z.B. `https://checkion-mcp.deine-domain.de`):

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "checkion": {
      "command": "uvx",
      "args": [
        "mcp-proxy",
        "--transport",
        "streamablehttp",
        "https://DEINE-MCP-SERVER-URL"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

**Wenn der MCP-Server nur lokal läuft** (z.B. `npm run dev` im `mcp-server`-Ordner):

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "checkion": {
      "command": "uvx",
      "args": [
        "mcp-proxy",
        "--transport",
        "streamablehttp",
        "http://localhost:3100"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

Bei lokaler Nutzung: MCP-Server vor dem Öffnen von Claude starten (im Terminal `npm run dev` im Projektordner `mcp-server`).

**4. Claude komplett beenden und neu starten** (nicht nur Fenster schließen).

**5. Prüfen:** In Claude das Hammer-Symbol (🔨) anklicken – dort sollten die CHECKION-Tools erscheinen.

---

## Option 2: Server mit **stdio** (ohne Proxy)

Wir könnten den CHECKION-MCP-Server so erweitern, dass er neben HTTP auch **stdio** anbietet. Dann würde Claude ihn direkt starten (z.B. mit `node` + Pfad zur `dist/index.js` und `env` für URL/Token). Dafür müsste ich den Server-Code anpassen; wenn du das möchtest, sag kurz Bescheid.

---

**Config-Datei-Pfade:**

- **macOS:** `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json`  
- **Windows:** `%APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json`

**Wichtig:** Der CHECKION-API-Token liegt nur im MCP-Server (Umgebungsvariablen auf deinem Rechner oder in Coolify). In der Claude-Config brauchst du **keinen** Token – nur die Server-URL (bei Option 1 die Proxy-URL wie oben).

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So connect() does call await this._transport.start(). So our stdio transport will get start() called. Good. Now let me build and run a quick test with stdio. First, fix the log() in main - when USE_STDIO is false we're writing to process.stdout for log(); that could mix with the HTTP server's console.log. Let me keep log() as stderr for the "not configured" warning in HTTP mode too, so we don't change behavior. Actually in HTTP mode the current code used console.log - so the server logs to stdout. Let me leave log() as: in stdio mode use stderr (so we don't pollute the JSON-RPC stream), in HTTP mode use stdout (current behavior). Good.

Now add an npm script so the user can run the server in stdio mode easily, e.g. `npm run start:stdio` that sets MCP_TRANSPORT=stdio and runs node dist/index.js. Then in the README we document the Claude config with command "node" and args [path to dist/index.js] and env.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


Hier die Änderungen und wie du es in Claude einrichtest:

---

## Umgesetzt

1. **Stdio-Transport**  
   Wenn `MCP_TRANSPORT=stdio` gesetzt ist, nutzt der MCP-Server stdin/stdout statt HTTP. Logs gehen nach stderr, damit der JSON-RPC-Strom auf stdout ungestört bleibt.

2. **Einstieg im Code**  
   - `mcp-server/src/index.ts`: Abfrage von `MCP_TRANSPORT`; bei `stdio` wird `StdioServerTransport` verwendet, sonst wie bisher der HTTP-Server.

3. **Script**  
   - `npm run start:stdio` startet den Server im Stdio-Modus (setzt `MCP_TRANSPORT=stdio`).

4. **Doku**  
   - `mcp-server/README.md`: Abschnitt „Claude Desktop“ mit Konfigurationsbeispiel.  
   - `knowledge/checkion-mcp-server.md`: Hinweis auf Stdio und Claude Desktop.

---

## So bindest du es in Claude Desktop ein

1. **Einmal bauen** (falls noch nicht geschehen):
   ```bash
   cd mcp-server && npm install && npm run build
   ```

2. **Claude-Konfiguration öffnen**  
   Claude Desktop → **Einstellungen** → **Developer** → **Edit Config**.

3. **Folgenden Block eintragen** (Pfad und Token anpassen):

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "checkion": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["/Users/christoph.bordeck/Desktop/ANTIGRAVITY/CHECKION-1/mcp-server/dist/index.js"],
      "env": {
        "MCP_TRANSPORT": "stdio",
        "CHECKION_API_URL": "https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech",
        "CHECKION_API_TOKEN": "checkion_DEIN_TOKEN_AUS_EINSTELLUNGEN"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Wenn schon andere MCP-Server in `mcpServers` stehen, nur den Block `"checkion": { ... }` zusätzlich einfügen.

4. **Claude Desktop vollständig beenden** (z. B. per Rechtsklick auf das Dock-Symbol → Beenden) und wieder starten.

5. **Prüfen**  
   In Claude das Hammer-Symbol (🔨) öffnen – dort sollten die CHECKION-Tools erscheinen (z. B. `checkion/health`, `checkion/projects_list`, `checkion/scan_single`).

Die Meldungen zu Tool-Namen mit „/“ (z. B. `checkion/scan_single`) sind nur Hinweise des MCP-SDK; die Tools funktionieren damit. Wenn du möchtest, können wir die Namen später auf ein anderes Format (z. B. mit Bindestrich) umstellen.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is asking whether they always need to build the MCP server first, or if the config alone is enough. They thought just the config would suffice.

The current setup requires:
1. `npm run build` in mcp-server to compile TypeScript to JavaScript (dist/index.js)
2. Claude config points to `dist/index.js` and runs it with `node`

So yes, currently they need to build first because we're shipping TypeScript source and the entry point we're telling Claude to run is the compiled `dist/index.js`. If we didn't build, that file wouldn't exist or would be stale.

Options to make it "just config":
1. **Use `tsx` (or `ts-node`) in the config** so Claude runs the TypeScript directly: `command: "npx", args: ["tsx", "path/to/mcp-server/src/index.ts"]` - then no build step needed. Requires tsx to be installed (we have it as devDependency in mcp-server). But when Claude runs `npx tsx ...` it needs to run from a directory that has node_modules (the mcp-server folder) so that tsx and the SDK are available. So we could do: `command: "npx", args: ["tsx", "src/index.ts"], "cwd": "/path/to/mcp-server"` or run from mcp-server with npx tsx src/index.ts. Let me check if Claude config supports cwd - I'm not sure. Alternatively we could use `node` with a path that runs from mcp-server: `node dist/index.js` from the mcp-server directory - but then we need to set cwd so that node can resolve any relative paths. Actually our server doesn't use relative path requires for the SDK - it's all from node_modules. So when we run `node /absolute/path/to/dist/index.js`, Node's process.cwd() might be something else (e.g. Claude's app directory). Our code only uses process.env and in-memory stuff, no __dirname reads. So running node with absolute path to dist/index.js should work. The issue is just that dist/ has to exist = build required.

To avoid build: use `npx tsx /path/to/mcp-server/src/index.ts`. But npx needs to find tsx - if we run from Claude's cwd, npx might use a global tsx or install it. And the script has `import ... from '@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/...'` - so when tsx runs the script, the script's working directory matters for resolving node_modules. In Node/tsx, when you run `node /path/to/file.js`, the module resolution starts from the file's directory. So actually when we do `npx tsx /absolute/path/to/mcp-server/src/index.ts`, tsx will run that file and node_modules resolution typically looks in the file's directory and parent directories. So the mcp-server's node_modules would be at /absolute/path/to/mcp-server/node_modules - and the file is in mcp-server/src/, so parent is mcp-server which has node_modules. So it should work! Let me suggest using tsx in the Claude config so they don't need to build. We need to make sure the config runs from a context where mcp-server's node_modules is found. When you execute `npx tsx /path/to/mcp-server/src/index.ts`, the current working directory might be Claude's. Node module resolution for the entry point's imports: Node resolves from the directory of the script. So for /path/to/mcp-server/src/index.ts, resolution might be from /path/to/mcp-server/src first, then parent. So node_modules in mcp-server would be found. I'll recommend using npx tsx with the path to src/index.ts so no build is needed, and update the README.
</details></think>

So the process would exit because main() returns! That's the bug. In stdio mode the process must keep running to read from stdin. Let me look at the code again - when we connect the transport and then return from main(), the process has nothing to do and will exit. The StdioServerTransport listens on stdin - but that's set up when we connect. The Protocol's connect() calls transport.start() which starts listening on stdin. So the event loop should have an active listener on stdin and the process should not exit. Unless... when running from /tmp, maybe something is different. Let me check - in our index we do:
  if (USE_STDIO) {

    await mcpServer.connect(transport);
    ...
    log('Running in stdio mode (Claude Desktop).');
    return;
  }
So we return from main(). The process will exit unless there's something keeping the event loop alive. The stdin listener - in Node, if you're listening to stdin, does that keep the process alive? Yes, typically open streams (stdin) with listeners keep the process running. So it should stay alive. Unless the way we're running it (with head?) closed stdin immediately and the process got an end event and exited. Let me not worry - in real use Claude will keep stdin open. _**User**_

wieso muss denn überhaupt was auf dem claude desktop system liegen? 

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is asking why anything has to be installed or lie on the Claude Desktop system at all. They're questioning the architecture: why can't Claude just connect to a remote MCP server (e.g. on Coolify) without having the server code or node/tsx on their machine?

The answer is: **Claude Desktop's built-in MCP support only works with "local" servers that it starts itself via a command (stdio)**. It doesn't support connecting to a remote HTTP/Streamable HTTP URL out of the box. So either:

1. **Local (current approach)**: Claude runs a command (e.g. `npx tsx ...` or `node dist/index.js`) on the user's machine. That process uses stdio to talk to Claude. The CHECKION API token and URL are in the env; the local process then calls the remote CHECKION API. So the "server" runs locally, but it's just a thin client that forwards to CHECKION.

2. **Remote**: The MCP server runs elsewhere (e.g. Coolify at `https://checkion-mcp.example.com`). Then Claude would need to connect to that URL. But Claude Desktop doesn't support that natively – you'd need a proxy like `mcp-proxy` that Claude starts locally (stdio), and the proxy connects to the remote HTTP URL. So you'd still run something locally (the proxy), but the actual MCP server code runs on Coolify.

So the user might be asking: "Why do I need the repo/code on my machine at all? Can't the MCP server just run on Coolify and Claude connect to it?"

If they deploy the MCP server on Coolify (Streamable HTTP), then:
- Claude Desktop **cannot** directly point to that URL in its config (it only supports command + args for stdio).
- They would need to use **mcp-proxy** (Option 1 from earlier): Claude runs `uvx mcp-proxy --transport streamablehttp https://checkion-mcp.coolify.example.com`. Then nothing from the CHECKION repo needs to be on their machine – only Python/uv for `uvx` and the proxy. The actual MCP server runs in the cloud.

So the answer is: **It doesn't have to.** If the MCP server runs on Coolify (or anywhere with a public URL), you only need a small local bridge (mcp-proxy) that Claude starts. Then no CHECKION code or node/tsx needs to be on the Claude Desktop system. I'll explain both options clearly and suggest documenting the "remote only" option in the README.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


Es muss nichts auf dem Claude-Desktop-System liegen.

Claude Desktop startet nur lokale Prozesse (über `command` + `args`) und spricht mit ihnen per stdio — es kann keine Remote-URL eintragen. Zwei Wege:

- Alles lokal: Du hast das Repo/`mcp-server` auf dem Rechner, Claude startet `npx tsx …` oder `node dist/index.js`. Der Prozess läuft lokal und ruft nur die CHECKION-API (remote) auf.
- Nur Proxy lokal, Server remote: Der MCP-Server läuft z. B. auf Coolify unter einer URL. Auf dem Rechner läuft nur ein kleines Programm (z. B. `mcp-proxy`), das Claude per stdio startet und das mit der Remote-URL spricht. Dann brauchst du kein CHECKION-Repo und kein Node/tsx auf dem Claude-System.

README um die "nur Remote"-Variante ergänzen.

Kurz die Antwort und was ich geändert habe:

---

**Warum überhaupt etwas auf dem Claude-Desktop-System?**

Weil Claude Desktop **keine reine Remote-URL** unterstützt. Er kann nur einen **lokalen Befehl starten** (z.B. `uvx …` oder `npx tsx …`) und mit diesem Prozess per stdio reden. Einen Eintrag wie „Verbinde dich mit https://…“ gibt es nicht.

**Zwei Wege:**

1. **Variante A (nichts vom Projekt auf deinem Rechner)**  
   - MCP-Server läuft **nur remote** (z.B. auf Coolify) mit `CHECKION_API_URL` und `CHECKION_API_TOKEN`.  
   - Auf dem Rechner mit Claude: **nur** [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) (für `uvx`) installieren und in der Config den **Proxy** eintragen:
   - `command`: `uvx`, `args`: `["mcp-proxy", "--transport", "streamablehttp", "https://DEINE-MCP-SERVER-URL"]`  
   - Dann liegt **kein** CHECKION-Repo, kein Node, kein Build auf dem Claude-System – nur dieser Proxy-Aufruf, der zur Remote-URL verbindet.

2. **Variante B (Repo liegt eh auf dem Rechner)**  
   - Du startest den MCP-Server **lokal** (z.B. `npx tsx …/mcp-server/src/index.ts`) und in der Config stehen Pfad + Token.  
   - Dann muss das Projekt (oder zumindest `mcp-server` + `npm install`) auf dem Rechner sein.

Im README steht jetzt **Variante A** als empfohlene Option zuerst und am Ende der Abschnitt: *„Warum überhaupt etwas lokal? Claude Desktop unterstützt nur stdio (Befehl starten). Bei Variante A startet Claude nur den Proxy – auf deinem System liegt nichts vom CHECKION-Projekt.“*

            "isSignature": true,
            "lineNumber": 290,
            "text": "async def run_agent(job_id: str, url: str, task: str) -\u003e None:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 291,
            "text": "    try:"

            "text": "    except ImportError as e:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 294,

          },

          {
            "lineNumber": 296,
            "text": "            if j:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 297,
            "text": "                j.status = \"error\""
          },

          },

            "lineNumber": 299,
            "text": "        return"
          },

            "lineNumber": 300
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 301,
            "text": "    async with _jobs_lock:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 302,
            "text": "        if job_id in _jobs:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 303,
            "text": "            _jobs[job_id].status = \"running\""
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 304
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 305,
            "text": "    browser = None"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 306,

            "text": "    video_dir = str(VIDEO_BASE_DIR / job_id)"
          },

          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 309
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 310,
            "text": "    try:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 311,
            "text": "        llm = _make_llm()"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 312,
            "text": "        try:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 313,
            "text": "            browser = Browser(record_video_dir=video_dir)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 314,
            "text": "        except TypeError:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 315,
            "text": "            browser = Browser()"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 316,
            "text": "        # Prefer initial_url if supported; else bake URL into task. Instruct model to output reasoning in German."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 317,
            "text": "        import inspect"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 318,
            "text": "        sig = inspect.signature(Agent.__init__)"
          },

            "text": "        german_instruction = \"WICHTIG: Formuliere alle deine Überlegungen und Gedanken (thinking/reasoning) ausschließlich auf Deutsch. \""

          {
            "lineNumber": 320,
            "text": "        task_with_lang = german_instruction + task"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 321,
            "text": "        agent_kw: dict[str, Any] = {\"task\": task_with_lang, \"llm\": llm, \"browser\": browser}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 322,
            "text": "        if \"initial_url\" in sig.parameters:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 323,
            "text": "            agent_kw[\"initial_url\"] = url"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 324,
            "text": "        else:"
          },
          {

            "text": "            agent_kw[\"task\"] = f\"Go to {url}. Then: {task_with_lang}\""

          {
            "lineNumber": 326,

          },
          {

          {
            "lineNumber": 328,
            "text": "        if hasattr(agent, \"max_steps\"):"

            "lineNumber": 329,

          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 330
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 331,

          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 332,
            "text": "            # Pause at the beginning of each step so the video shows the current state before the action runs"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 333,
            "text": "            await asyncio.sleep(max(0, STEP_START_DELAY_SECONDS))"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 334
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 335,
            "text": "        async def _on_step_end(agent_instance: Any) -\u003e None:"

          {
            "lineNumber": 336,
            "text": "            actions: list[Any] = []"
          }
        ],

          {
            "isSignature": true,
            "lineNumber": 111,
            "text": "export async function runScan(options: ScanOptions \u0026 { groupId?: string; targetRegion?: string }): Promise\u003cScanResult\u003e {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 256
          },

            "lineNumber": 259
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 260,
            "text": "            let currentScroll = 0;"

            "lineNumber": 261
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 262,
            "text": "            // Scroll Down"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 263,
            "text": "            while ((window.innerHeight + currentScroll) \u003c totalHeight) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 264,
            "text": "                window.scrollBy(0, distance);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 265,
            "text": "                currentScroll += distance;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 266,
            "text": "                await new Promise((resolve) =\u003e setTimeout(resolve, delay));"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 267,
            "text": "                // Cap to avoid infinite loops"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 268,
            "text": "                if (currentScroll \u003e 5000) break;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 269,
            "text": "            }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 270,
            "text": "            // Scroll Up Trigger"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 271,

          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 272,
            "text": "        });"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 273,
            "text": "        // Allow time for shifts to settle"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 274,
            "text": "        await new Promise((r) =\u003e setTimeout(r, 1000));"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 275
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 276,
            "text": "        // Re-apply cookie banner hide before screenshot (in case of late-loading banners)"
          },
          {

            "text": "        await dismissCookieBanner(page);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 278
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 279,
            "text": "        // --- Extract Performance Metrics (incl. LCP/INP from observers) ---"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 280,
            "text": "        const perfData = await page.evaluate(function () {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 281,
            "text": "            const navHeading = performance.getEntriesByType('navigation')[0] as PerformanceNavigationTiming;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 282,
            "text": "            const paint = performance.getEntriesByName('first-contentful-paint')[0];"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 283,
            "text": "            const ttfb = navHeading ? (navHeading.responseStart - navHeading.requestStart) : 0;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 284,
            "text": "            const domLoad = navHeading ? (navHeading.domContentLoadedEventEnd - navHeading.fetchStart) : 0;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 285,
            "text": "            const windowLoad = navHeading ? (navHeading.loadEventEnd - navHeading.fetchStart) : 0;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 286,
            "text": "            const fcp = paint ? paint.startTime : 0;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 287,
            "text": "            const lcp = (window as any).__lcp_value != null ? Math.round((window as any).__lcp_value) : 0;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 288,
            "text": "            const inp = (window as any).__inp_value != null ? Math.round((window as any).__inp_value) : null;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 289,
            "text": "            return {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 290,
            "text": "                ttfb: Math.round(ttfb),"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 291,
            "text": "                fcp: Math.round(fcp),"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 292,
            "text": "                domLoad: Math.round(domLoad),"

          {
            "lineNumber": 293,

          {
            "lineNumber": 294,
            "text": "                lcp,"

            "lineNumber": 295,
            "text": "                inp"
          },
          {

            "text": "        });"
          },

            "lineNumber": 298
          },

Die Analyse steht in **`knowledge/checkion-api-mcp-audit.md`**. Hier die kompakte Übersicht und konkrete Vorschläge.

---

## Bestand

**API (Auszug):**
- **Auth/User:** Profile (GET/PATCH), Passwort ändern, Tokens (List/Create/Revoke)
- **Projekte:** CRUD (List, Get, Create, PATCH, DELETE) + Zuweisungen (Scan/Domain/Journey/GEO → Projekt)
- **Scans:** Single (POST/GET/List + project/summarize/screenshot), Domain (POST/Status/Summary/Summarize/Journey/List/Delete/Project), Journey-Agent, GEO/E-E-A-T inkl. Suggest, History, Rerun, Competitive-History
- **Tools (einzelne Checks):** `contrast`, `extract`, `readability`, `ssl-labs`, `pagespeed`, `wayback`
- **Weitere:** Journeys (CRUD), Share (Create/Get/Revoke/ByResource/Access/…), Search, Saliency

**MCP (13 Tools):**
- Scans: `scan_single`, `scan_get`, `scans_list`, `scan_domain`, `scan_domain_status`, `scan_domain_summary`, `scans_domain_list`
- Projekte: `projects_list`, `project_get`, `project_create`
- Tools: `tools_contrast`, `tools_extract`
- Sonst: `health`

---

## Lücken und Empfehlungen

### 1. Kleinteilige Checks

- **API:** Die einzelnen Checks sind schon da: `/api/tools/contrast`, `extract`, `readability`, `ssl-labs`, `pagespeed`, `wayback`.
- **MCP:** Es fehlen die Tools zu den bestehenden Endpunkten: **SSL, PageSpeed, Wayback, Readability**.
- **Optional:** Zusätzliche **einheitliche** Endpunkte unter z. B. **`/api/checks/…`** (z. B. `/api/checks/ssl`, `/api/checks/pagespeed`, …), die intern die gleiche Logik wie die Tools nutzen und ein einheitliches Antwortformat liefern. Dann können UI und MCP konsequent „Checks“ über einen Namensraum abbilden.

### 2. User-Management

- **API:** Ausreichend (Profil, Passwort, Tokens).
- **MCP:** Kein User-Zugriff. Vorschlag: **`checkion/user_profile`** (read-only) → GET `/api/auth/profile`. Kein Passwort/Registrierung im MCP.

### 3. Projekt-Management

- **API:** CRUD ist vollständig.
- **MCP:** Es fehlen **`checkion/project_update`** (PATCH) und **`checkion/project_delete`** (DELETE).

### 4. Weitere sinnvolle MCP-Tools (ohne neue API-Logik)

- **Projekt-Zuweisungen:**  
  `scan_assign_project`, `scan_domain_assign_project`, `scan_journey_assign_project`, `scan_geo_eeat_assign_project` (jeweils PATCH auf die bestehenden Projekt-Routen).
- **Journeys:**  
  `journeys_list`, `journey_get`, `journey_delete`.
- **GEO/E-E-A-T:**  
  `geo_eeat_suggest_queries`, `geo_eeat_history`, `geo_eeat_get`, `geo_eeat_rerun_competitive`.
- **Suche:**  
  `search` (GET `/api/search?q=...`).
- **Share (optional):**  
  `share_create`, `share_get`, `share_revoke`.

---

## Vorschlag für die nächsten Schritte

1. **MCP erweitern (ohne neue API):**
   - Checks: `tools_ssl`, `tools_pagespeed`, `tools_wayback`, `tools_readability`.
   - User: `user_profile`.
   - Projekte: `project_update`, `project_delete`.
   - Zuweisungen: die vier `*_assign_project`-Tools.
   - Journeys: `journeys_list`, `journey_get`, `journey_delete`.
   - GEO: `geo_eeat_suggest_queries`, `geo_eeat_history`, `geo_eeat_get`, `geo_eeat_rerun_competitive`.
   - Suche: `search`.
   - Optional: Share-Tools.

2. **Optional – kleinteilige Check-API:**
   - Neue Routen unter **`/api/checks/…`** (z. B. `ssl`, `pagespeed`, `wayback`, `readability`, `contrast`), die die bestehende Tool-Logik wiederverwenden und ein einheitliches Response-Schema liefern (z. B. `{ type, score, details }`). MCP könnte dann entweder weiter die `tools_*`-Endpunkte nutzen oder auf `checks_*` umgestellt werden.

Wenn du sagst, ob du zuerst nur MCP ausbauen willst oder auch die `/api/checks/*`-Endpunkte, kann ich dir als Nächstes eine konkrete Implementierungsliste (Dateien + Änderungen) vorschlagen.

{
  "name": "API Checks + MCP Erweiterung",
  "overview": "Zwei Teile: (1) Neue API-Routen unter &#96;/api/checks/*&#96; als einheitlicher Namensraum für kleinteilige Checks (Proxy zu bestehenden &#96;/api/tools/*&#96;). (2) Vollständige Erweiterung der MCP-Tools um User, Projekt-CRUD, Zuweisungen, Journeys, GEO/E-E-A-T, Suche und Share.",
  "plan": "# Plan: Kleinteilige Check-API + vollständige MCP-Erweiterung\n\n## Teil 1: API unter &#96;/api/checks/*&#96;\n\nEinheitlicher Namensraum für Checks; keine Duplizierung der Logik: jede Route leitet an die bestehende Tool-API weiter und gibt deren Antwort unverändert zurück.\n\n**Zu erstellende Dateien (je GET bzw. POST forward):**\n\n| Pfad | Methode | Forward-Ziel |\n|------|---------|----------------|\n| [app/api/checks/contrast/route.ts](app/api/checks/contrast/route.ts) | GET | &#96;/api/tools/contrast&#96; (Query: f, b) |\n| [app/api/checks/ssl/route.ts](app/api/checks/ssl/route.ts) | GET | &#96;/api/tools/ssl-labs&#96; (Query: host) |\n| [app/api/checks/pagespeed/route.ts](app/api/checks/pagespeed/route.ts) | GET | &#96;/api/tools/pagespeed&#96; (Query: url) |\n| [app/api/checks/wayback/route.ts](app/api/checks/wayback/route.ts) | GET | &#96;/api/tools/wayback&#96; (Query: url) |\n| [app/api/checks/readability/route.ts](app/api/checks/readability/route.ts) | POST | &#96;/api/tools/readability&#96; (Body: { text }) |\n\n**Implementierung:** In jeder Route: &#96;origin&#96; aus &#96;new URL(request.url).origin&#96;, Ziel-URL bauen (Pfad + gleiche Query/Body), &#96;fetch(zielUrl, { method, headers, body })&#96;, Response (Status + Body) durchreichen. Bei GET Query-Parameter der Request-URL an &#96;/api/tools/...&#96; anhängen; bei POST Body durchreichen und &#96;Content-Type: application/json&#96; setzen.\n\n**Konstanten:** In [lib/constants.ts](lib/constants.ts) optionale Einträge für die Check-Pfade (z. B. &#96;API_CHECKS_SSL&#96;, …), falls ihr sie zentral referenzieren wollt; für die reine Proxy-Logik nicht zwingend nötig.\n\n---\n\n## Teil 2: MCP-Tools erweitern\n\nAlle neuen Tools in [mcp-server/src/tools.ts](mcp-server/src/tools.ts) registrieren; Aufrufe wie bisher über &#96;checkionFetch&#96; (Bearer-Token) an die CHECKION-API.\n\n### 2.1 Checks (bestehende Tools-API)\n\n| MCP-Tool | API | Parameter |\n|----------|-----|-----------|\n| &#96;checkion/tools_ssl&#96; | GET &#96;/api/tools/ssl-labs?host=&#96; | host (string) |\n| &#96;checkion/tools_pagespeed&#96; | GET &#96;/api/tools/pagespeed?url=&#96; | url (string) |\n| &#96;checkion/tools_wayback&#96; | GET &#96;/api/tools/wayback?url=&#96; | url (string) |\n| &#96;checkion/tools_readability&#96; | POST &#96;/api/tools/readability&#96; | text (string) |\n\n### 2.2 User (read-only)\n\n| MCP-Tool | API |\n|----------|-----|\n| &#96;checkion/user_profile&#96; | GET &#96;/api/auth/profile&#96; |\n\n### 2.3 Projekte (fehlende CRUD-Teiloperationen)\n\n| MCP-Tool | API | Parameter |\n|----------|-----|-----------|\n| &#96;checkion/project_update&#96; | PATCH &#96;/api/projects/[id]&#96; | id, name?, domain? |\n| &#96;checkion/project_delete&#96; | DELETE &#96;/api/projects/[id]&#96; | id |\n\n### 2.4 Projekt-Zuweisungen\n\nBody bei allen: &#96;{ projectId: string | null }&#96;. Schema: [projectAssignmentBodySchema](lib/api-schemas.ts) (projectId uuid oder null).\n\n| MCP-Tool | API |\n|----------|-----|\n| &#96;checkion/scan_assign_project&#96; | PATCH &#96;/api/scan/[id]/project&#96; (id = scanId) |\n| &#96;checkion/scan_domain_assign_project&#96; | PATCH &#96;/api/scans/domain/[id]/project&#96; |\n| &#96;checkion/scan_journey_assign_project&#96; | PATCH &#96;/api/scan/journey-agent/[jobId]/project&#96; |\n| &#96;checkion/scan_geo_eeat_assign_project&#96; | PATCH &#96;/api/scan/geo-eeat/[jobId]/project&#96; |\n\n### 2.5 Journeys (gespeicherte Journeys)\n\n| MCP-Tool | API | Parameter |\n|----------|-----|-----------|\n| &#96;checkion/journeys_list&#96; | GET &#96;/api/journeys?limit=\u0026page=&#96; | limit?, page? |\n| &#96;checkion/journey_get&#96; | GET &#96;/api/journeys/[id]&#96; | id |\n| &#96;checkion/journey_delete&#96; | DELETE &#96;/api/journeys/[id]&#96; | id |\n\nPOST „Journey speichern“ (Create) optional später; aktuell nur List/Get/Delete.\n\n### 2.6 GEO / E-E-A-T\n\n| MCP-Tool | API | Parameter |\n|----------|-----|-----------|\n| &#96;checkion/geo_eeat_suggest_queries&#96; | POST &#96;/api/scan/geo-eeat/suggest-competitors-queries&#96; | Body (url, optional weitere Felder laut Route) |\n| &#96;checkion/geo_eeat_history&#96; | GET &#96;/api/scan/geo-eeat/history?limit=\u0026projectId=&#96; | limit?, projectId? |\n| &#96;checkion/geo_eeat_get&#96; | GET &#96;/api/scan/geo-eeat/[jobId]&#96; | jobId |\n| &#96;checkion/geo_eeat_rerun_competitive&#96; | POST &#96;/api/scan/geo-eeat/[jobId]/rerun-competitive&#96; | jobId, ggf. Body falls Route welchen erwartet |\n\nBodies/Schemas der GEO-Routen in [app/api/scan/geo-eeat/suggest-competitors-queries/route.ts](app/api/scan/geo-eeat/suggest-competitors-queries/route.ts) und [app/api/scan/geo-eeat/[jobId]/rerun-competitive/route.ts](app/api/scan/geo-eeat/[jobId]/rerun-competitive/route.ts) prüfen und in den MCP-Tools abbilden.\n\n### 2.7 Suche\n\n| MCP-Tool | API | Parameter |\n|----------|-----|-----------|\n| &#96;checkion/search&#96; | GET &#96;/api/search?q=\u0026limit=&#96; | q (string), limit? (number) |\n\n### 2.8 Share (optional, aber im Plan enthalten)\n\n| MCP-Tool | API | Parameter |\n|----------|-----|-----------|\n| &#96;checkion/share_create&#96; | POST &#96;/api/share&#96; | type (single \\| domain \\| journey \\| geo_eeat), id, password? |\n| &#96;checkion/share_get&#96; | GET &#96;/api/share/[token]&#96; | token |\n| &#96;checkion/share_revoke&#96; | DELETE &#96;/api/share/[token]&#96; | token |\n\n---\n\n## Ablauf (Reihenfolge)\n\n1. **API Checks:** Fünf Route-Dateien unter &#96;app/api/checks/&#96; anlegen (contrast, ssl, pagespeed, wayback, readability) mit Forward-Logik wie oben. Optional [lib/constants.ts](lib/constants.ts) um Check-Pfade ergänzen.\n2. **MCP:** In [mcp-server/src/tools.ts](mcp-server/src/tools.ts) alle neuen Tools nacheinander registrieren (inputSchema mit zod, Handler ruft &#96;base(...)&#96; mit passendem Pfad/Method/Body/Query auf, Fehler wie bisher über &#96;isCheckionError&#96; und text content zurückgeben).\n3. **Dokumentation:** [mcp-server/README.md](mcp-server/README.md) Tool-Tabelle um die neuen Einträge erweitern; [knowledge/checkion-api-mcp-audit.md](knowledge/checkion-api-mcp-audit.md) auf „umgesetzt“ aktualisieren (Check-API + MCP-Erweiterung).\n4. **Tests:** Mindestens einen kurzen Test für einen Check-Proxy (z. B. GET &#96;/api/checks/contrast?f=000\u0026b=fff&#96; leitet an tools/contrast weiter und liefert 200 mit ratio) und einen MCP-relevanten Aufruf (z. B. user_profile oder project_update), sofern ihr dafür Tests habt (z. B. in &#96;__tests__/&#96; oder Playwright).\n\n---\n\n## Kurzüberblick\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  subgraph api [API]\n    checks[\"/api/checks/*\"]\n    tools[\"/api/tools/*\"]\n    auth[\"/api/auth/profile\"]\n    projects[\"/api/projects\"]\n    scan[\"/api/scan\"]\n    journeys[\"/api/journeys\"]\n    geo[\"/api/scan/geo-eeat\"]\n    search[\"/api/search\"]\n    share[\"/api/share\"]\n  end\n  subgraph mcp [MCP Tools]\n    t_checks[tools_ssl, pagespeed, wayback, readability]\n    t_user[user_profile]\n    t_proj[project_update, project_delete, assign_project x4]\n    t_jour[journeys_list, journey_get, journey_delete]\n    t_geo[geo_eeat_suggest, history, get, rerun]\n    t_search[search]\n    t_share[share_create, get, revoke]\n  end\n  checks --\u003e|forward| tools\n  t_checks --\u003e tools\n  t_user --\u003e auth\n  t_proj --\u003e projects\n  t_proj --\u003e scan\n  t_jour --\u003e journeys\n  t_geo --\u003e geo\n  t_search --\u003e search\n  t_share --\u003e share\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nKeine Änderungen an bestehenden Tool-Implementierungen (z. B. [app/api/tools/contrast/route.ts](app/api/tools/contrast/route.ts)); nur neue Routen und MCP-Registrierungen.\n"
}

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The health test imports the route handler directly and calls GET(). For the checks proxy we need to call GET(request) with a NextRequest that has a url. Let me check how NextRequest is constructed in tests - we need a request with a URL that has an origin so the proxy can build the target. Actually the proxy does fetch(origin + '/api/tools/contrast?...'). So when we run the test, the request.url could be 'http://localhost:3000/api/checks/contrast?f=000&b=fff'. Then the proxy will fetch 'http://localhost:3000/api/tools/contrast?f=000&b=fff'. But that fetch will run in Node and hit the same process - so we need the app to be running or we need to mock fetch. Alternatively we could test that the route returns 200 and the response has the expected shape when we mock fetch. The simplest approach: mock global fetch to return a known response, then call the check route and assert it returns that response with status 200. Let me do that.
</details></think>

Die geplanten Änderungen sind umgesetzt. Kurzüberblick:

---

## 1. API unter `/api/checks/*`

- **5 neue Routen** (Proxy auf `/api/tools/*`):
  - `app/api/checks/contrast/route.ts` (GET)
  - `app/api/checks/ssl/route.ts` (GET)
  - `app/api/checks/pagespeed/route.ts` (GET)
  - `app/api/checks/wayback/route.ts` (GET)
  - `app/api/checks/readability/route.ts` (POST)
- In **`lib/constants.ts`** wurden die Check-Pfade ergänzt (`API_CHECKS_CONTRAST`, `API_CHECKS_SSL`, …).

---

## 2. MCP-Tools

In **`mcp-server/src/tools.ts`** wurden alle geplanten Tools ergänzt:

- **Checks:** `tools_ssl`, `tools_pagespeed`, `tools_wayback`, `tools_readability`
- **User:** `user_profile`
- **Projekte:** `project_update`, `project_delete`
- **Zuweisungen:** `scan_assign_project`, `scan_domain_assign_project`, `scan_journey_assign_project`, `scan_geo_eeat_assign_project`
- **Journeys:** `journeys_list`, `journey_get`, `journey_delete`
- **GEO/E-E-A-T:** `geo_eeat_suggest_queries`, `geo_eeat_history`, `geo_eeat_get`, `geo_eeat_rerun_competitive`
- **Suche:** `search`
- **Share:** `share_create`, `share_get`, `share_revoke`

---

## 3. Doku

- **`mcp-server/README.md`:** Tool-Tabelle um alle neuen Tools ergänzt und nach Kategorien gegliedert.
- **`knowledge/checkion-api-mcp-audit.md`:** Abschnitt zu `/api/checks/*` ergänzt, MCP-Liste und „Lücken“ auf „umgesetzt“ gestellt.

---

## 4. Tests

- **`__tests__/api/checks-contrast.test.ts`:** Zwei Fälle für den Check-Proxy (Erfolg mit Weiterleitung, Weiterleitung von 400).
- **`__tests__/api/auth-profile.test.ts`:** Ein Fall für GET `/api/auth/profile` (401 ohne Auth, für MCP `user_profile` relevant).

Beide neuen Tests laufen mit `npm run test` erfolgreich durch. MCP-Build (`npm run build` im `mcp-server`) baut fehlerfrei.

---

## Fehlende MCP-Tools (nach Priorität)

### Hohe Priorität (zentrale Flows)
| API | Vorschlag Tool |
|-----|------------------|
| **POST** `/api/scan/journey-agent` (Journey starten) | `checkion/scan_journey_start` |
| **GET** `/api/scan/journey-agent/[jobId]` (Journey-Ergebnis) | `checkion/scan_journey_get` |
| **GET** `/api/scan/journey-agent/history` (Journey-Runs auflisten) | `checkion/scan_journey_history` |
| **POST** `/api/scan/geo-eeat` (GEO/E-E-A-T starten) | `checkion/geo_eeat_start` |
| **GET** `/api/scan/geo-eeat/[jobId]/status` (Job-Status) | `checkion/geo_eeat_status` |

### Mittlere Priorität (CRUD & Share)
| API | Vorschlag Tool |
|-----|------------------|
| **GET** `.../competitive-history` (Durchläufe) | `checkion/geo_eeat_competitive_history` |
| **GET** `.../competitive-history/[runId]` (ein Durchlauf) | `checkion/geo_eeat_competitive_run_get` |
| **DELETE** `/api/scans/[id]` (Single-Scan löschen) | `checkion/scan_delete` |
| **DELETE** `/api/scans/domain/[id]` (Domain-Scan löschen) | `checkion/scan_domain_delete` |
| **GET** `/api/share/by-resource?type=&id=` (Share zu Resource) | `checkion/share_by_resource` |

### Niedrig / Nische
- `checkion/scan_summarize`, `checkion/scan_domain_summarize` (LLM-Summaries)
- `checkion/scan_domain_journey_start` (Journey aus Domain-Kontext starten)
- `checkion/journey_save` (POST `/api/journeys` – Journey speichern)
- `checkion/scan_screenshot` (Screenshot abrufen)
- optional: Saliency (`saliency_generate`, `saliency_result`)

---

**Bewusst ohne MCP-Tool** (sensibel): Registrierung, Passwort ändern, API-Tokens verwalten, Profil bearbeiten (PATCH).

Wenn du möchtest, kann ich als Nächstes die **hoch priorisierten** Tools (Journey Start/Get/History, GEO Start/Status, ggf. Competitive-History) im MCP-Server anlegen und in der README eintragen.

## Umgesetzte MCP-Tools

### Scans

- **`checkion/scan_domain_delete`** – Domain-Scan per ID löschen

- **`checkion/scan_journey_start`** – Journey-Lauf starten (`url`, `task`, optional `projectId`)  
- **`checkion/scan_journey_get`** – Journey-Ergebnis per `jobId` abrufen  
- **`checkion/scan_journey_history`** – Journey-Läufe auflisten (`limit`, `projectId`)  

### GEO / E-E-A-T
- **`checkion/geo_eeat_start`** – GEO/E-E-A-T-Scan starten (inkl. optionalem Competitive-Benchmark)  
- **`checkion/geo_eeat_status`** – Job-Status per `jobId` (z. B. zum Polling)  
- **`checkion/geo_eeat_competitive_history`** – Competitive-Durchläufe zu einem Job auflisten  
- **`checkion/geo_eeat_competitive_run_get`** – Ein Competitive-Durchlauf per `jobId` + `runId` abrufen  

### Share
- **`checkion/share_by_resource`** – Vorhandenen Share-Link zu einer Resource abfragen (`type`, `id`)

For scan_screenshot - the API returns binary (image). The MCP tool returns text content. So we have two options: (1) return a URL to the screenshot (the client would need to know the CHECKION base URL + path), or (2) fetch and return base64. The CHECKION API returns the image directly. When we call checkionFetch we get the response - if it's not JSON, we need to handle it. Let me check checkionFetch - it probably expects JSON. So for screenshot we could return a message with the URL to get the screenshot (same origin as CHECKION_API_URL + /api/scan/[id]/screenshot). That way the agent knows where to get the image. Or we could get the response as blob and return base64 in the text. I'll check the client.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

checkionFetch always does res.text() and JSON.parse. So for screenshot we get binary as text and JSON.parse will fail - we'd get the error branch. So for scan_screenshot the best we can do without changing the client is to return the URL where the screenshot can be fetched: `${CHECKION_API_URL}/api/scan/${id}/screenshot` (with Bearer token). I'll add a tool that returns that URL and a note that the image is at that endpoint with the same Bearer token. So the tool "scan_screenshot" can return a JSON like { screenshotUrl: "...", message: "Fetch with same Bearer token to get image" }. We need to get BASE_URL from somewhere - the client doesn't export it. We have process.env.CHECKION_API_URL in the client. So in tools we don't have it. We could add a tool that just returns the path and the user has to prepend base URL, or we could use a raw fetch in the tool to the same CHECKION API and if response is not JSON, return the URL. Actually the simplest is: the tool calls the API with a custom fetch that doesn't parse as JSON - when content-type is image, return a text message with the URL. But our base() uses checkionFetch which always expects JSON. So I have two options: 1) Add a helper in checkion-client that does fetch and returns { ok, contentType, bodyOrUrl } or 2) Just return the URL from the tool without calling the API (we have no access to CHECKION_API_URL in tools.ts). So we need to pass the base URL. It's in process.env in Node - process.env.CHECKION_API_URL is available in the MCP server when it runs. So in tools we can use process.env.CHECKION_API_URL. So the scan_screenshot tool can return text: `Screenshot URL (GET with same Bearer token): ${process.env.CHECKION_API_URL}/api/scan/${id}/screenshot`. That way the agent knows where to get the image. Let me implement that.
</details></think>

Alle restlichen MCP-Tools sind eingebaut. Kurzüberblick:

---

## Neu hinzugefügte MCP-Tools

### Scans
- **`checkion/scan_summarize`** – LLM-UX-Summary für einen Single-Page-Scan (id).
- **`checkion/scan_screenshot`** – Liefert die URL zum Screenshot (GET mit gleichem Bearer-Token für das Bild).
- **`checkion/scan_domain_summarize`** – LLM-Domain-Summary für einen abgeschlossenen Domain-Scan (id).
- **`checkion/scan_domain_journey_start`** – Journey aus Domain-Scan starten (id, goal, optional stream).

### Journeys (gespeichert)
- **`checkion/journey_save`** – Journey speichern (domainScanId, goal, result mit steps-Array, optional name).

### Saliency
- **`checkion/saliency_generate`** – Saliency-/Heatmap-Job starten (scanId), liefert jobId.
- **`checkion/saliency_result`** – Saliency-Ergebnis abrufen (jobId, scanId).

---

**Angepasste Dateien**
- `mcp-server/src/tools.ts` – 7 neue Tools.
- `mcp-server/README.md` – Tool-Tabelle und Abschnitt „Saliency“ ergänzt.
- `knowledge/checkion-api-mcp-audit.md` – Alle Einträge als umgesetzt markiert.

**Hinweise**
- **`scan_screenshot`**: Gibt nur die URL zurück (z. B. für Dokumentation oder Weiterverarbeitung). Das Bild muss per GET mit dem gleichen Bearer-Token geladen werden, da der MCP-Client nur Text zurückgibt.
- **`journey_save`**: `result` ist ein Objekt mit mindestens `steps` (Array); die API prüft das Format.

Build: `npm run build` im MCP-Server läuft fehlerfrei durch.

---

_**Agent (default)**_


Es gibt **zwei verschiedene URLs**:

---

## 1. MCP-Server-URL (wo der MCP-Server erreichbar ist)

Die **MCP-URL** ist die Adresse, an die sich Cursor oder ein anderer MCP-Client verbindet. Sie steht **nicht** im Code, sondern kommt von deinem Setup:

| Umgebung | MCP-URL |
|----------|---------|
| **Lokal** (HTTP-Modus) | `http://localhost:3100` (oder anderer Port über `MCP_PORT`) |
| **Coolify/Deployment** | Die öffentliche URL deines MCP-Services, z.B. `https://checkion-mcp.deine-domain.de` |

Requests gehen an die **Root-URL** (z.B. `https://checkion-mcp.deine-domain.de` oder `http://localhost:3100`), ohne Pfad wie `/mcp`.

---

## 2. CHECKION_API_URL (von der der MCP-Server die API aufruft)

Das ist die **CHECKION-App-URL**, die der MCP-Server als Backend nutzt – z.B.:

- `https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech`

Sie wird nur in den **Umgebungsvariablen** des MCP-Servers gesetzt (`CHECKION_API_URL`), nicht als „MCP-URL“ im Client.

---

**Kurz:**  
- **MCP-URL** = URL, die du in Cursor/Coolify für den MCP-Server einträgst (lokal: `http://localhost:3100`, deployed: deine öffentliche MCP-Domain).  
- **CHECKION_API_URL** = URL der CHECKION-App, die der MCP-Server intern verwendet.

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is saying that the MCP server is also deployed on Coolify, and the CHECKION app runs at https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech. They want to know how to reach the MCP server in that setup.

So they have:
- CHECKION app: https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech
- MCP server: also on Coolify - but the question is: is it the same container/app or a separate service? And what URL/path do you use to reach the MCP?

Typically in Coolify you'd deploy:
1. CHECKION (Next.js app) - one service, one domain e.g. checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech
2. MCP server - either a separate service with its own domain (e.g. checkion-mcp.projects-a.plygrnd.tech) or same domain with a path (e.g. checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp)

The CHECKION MCP server is a separate Node.js app (mcp-server folder) that listens on port 3100. So in Coolify you'd usually deploy it as a **separate application** and give it its own (sub)domain, e.g.:
- https://checkion-mcp.projects-a.plygrnd.tech
or a path-based setup if Coolify supports it:
- https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp (would require a reverse proxy to route /mcp to the MCP container)

So the answer is: If the MCP is a separate container on Coolify, it gets its **own public URL** in Coolify (e.g. you add a new application for the MCP server and assign a domain like checkion-mcp.projects-a.plygrnd.tech). You don't reach it "under" checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech unless you explicitly set up a reverse proxy/path (e.g. /mcp) that forwards to the MCP container.

I'll explain both options: (1) separate domain for MCP, (2) same domain with path if they want that (would need proxy config).
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Der MCP-Server ist ein **eigener Container** (Port 3100). Unter **https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech** läuft nur die CHECKION-App – der MCP-Server hat dort standardmäßig **keine** Route.

Du erreichst den MCP so:

---

## 1. Eigene Domain für den MCP (typisch in Coolify)

MCP in Coolify als **eigenes Application** deployen und eine eigene (Sub-)Domain zuweisen, z. B.:

- **https://checkion-mcp.projects-a.plygrnd.tech**

Dann ist das deine **MCP-URL** für Cursor / Claude (mcp-proxy):

```text
https://checkion-mcp.projects-a.plygrnd.tech
```

In Coolify:

1. Neues Application anlegen (gleiches Repo, Dockerfile: `mcp-server/Dockerfile`, Context z. B. `mcp-server/` oder Repo-Root).
2. Domain hinzufügen, z. B. **checkion-mcp.projects-a.plygrnd.tech** (oder eine andere Subdomain unter deiner Zone).
3. Env: `CHECKION_API_URL=https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech`, `CHECKION_API_TOKEN=checkion_…`, ggf. `MCP_PORT=3100`.
4. Port **3100** exponieren (in Coolify bei der App als Port 3100 einstellen, Coolify leitet die Domain darauf).

Dann erreichst du den MCP unter genau dieser Domain, **nicht** unter `https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech`.

---

## 2. MCP unter derselben Domain (z. B. `/mcp`)

Wenn der MCP **unter** https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech laufen soll, z. B.:

- **https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp**

müsstest du einen **Reverse-Proxy** einrichten, der z. B. `/mcp` an den MCP-Container (Port 3100) weiterleitet. Das geht z. B. mit:

- einem eigenen Proxy-Container (Traefik/Nginx) vor CHECKION und MCP, oder  
- Coolify-Proxy / Traefik-Routen, falls euer Setup das unterstützt.

Dann wäre die MCP-URL z. B. **https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp** (exakter Pfad hängt von der Proxy-Konfiguration ab).

---

## Kurz

- **Normale Coolify-Variante:** MCP als eigene App mit eigener Domain → MCP-URL = diese Domain, z. B. **https://checkion-mcp.projects-a.plygrnd.tech**.
- **Ohne eigene Domain:** Unter **https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech** erreichst du nur die CHECKION-App; den MCP erreichst du erst, wenn du ihm entweder eine eigene Domain gibst oder einen Proxy für einen Pfad (z. B. `/mcp`) einrichtest.

Wenn du sagst, ob du schon eine zweite Domain für den MCP in Coolify angelegt hast, kann man die konkrete URL und Cursor/Claude-Config darauf anpassen.

---

The user is asking if we can set up the proxy as part of the deployment - so that the MCP server is reachable under the same domain as CHECKION (e.g. https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp) via a reverse proxy configuration in the deployment/Coolify setup.

Options:
1. **Next.js rewrites** – The CHECKION app is Next.js. We could add a rewrite in next.config.js that proxies `/mcp` (or `/api/mcp`) to the MCP server (e.g. another container or same host on port 3100). That way one deployment (CHECKION) could expose both the app and the MCP under one domain.
2. **Coolify** – Coolify might allow adding multiple services under one application with path-based routing - that's a Coolify feature, not something we "code" in the repo. So we'd document it or use Coolify's config.
3. **Docker Compose / single deployment** – If they deploy with docker-compose, we could have nginx/traefik in front that routes / to CHECKION and /mcp to the MCP container. That would be in the repo as docker-compose + nginx config.

The most "we can do it in the repo" approach would be:
- **Next.js rewrites**: In the CHECKION Next.js app, add a rewrite in next.config.js that forwards requests from e.g. ` /mcp` or ` /api/mcp/*` to `http://localhost:3100` or `http://mcp-server:3100` (if MCP runs as sibling container). But wait - Next.js rewrites are for the Next.js server to fetch from another backend; the client would still request checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp and Next.js would proxy that to the MCP server. So the MCP would be at https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp. We need to check if the MCP Streamable HTTP transport expects to be at root or under a path. Looking at the MCP SDK - typically the client sends requests to a URL; if the server is at https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp then the client would use that as base. So we'd need the rewrite to forward /mcp to the MCP container. In Next.js, rewrites work like: request comes in for /mcp/something → we proxy to http://mcp:3100/mcp/something or http://mcp:3100/something? The MCP server listens on / and expects requests at root. So we'd need to proxy so that when client requests https://checkion.../mcp/ (or /mcp) the backend request goes to http://mcp:3100/ (strip /mcp prefix). Next.js rewrites have "pathRewrite" in some setups - in Next.js built-in rewrites we have `destination`. So if we set rewrite from /mcp to http://mcp:3100/, then request to /mcp/foo would go to http://mcp:3100/foo. Actually Next.js rewrite: `source: '/mcp/:path*', destination: 'http://mcp:3100/:path*'` - but Next.js doesn't support that for external URLs in the same way (destination is usually a path). Let me check - in Next.js, rewrites destination can be an external URL in some versions. So we could add:

```js
async rewrites() {
  return [
    { source: '/mcp', destination: 'http://localhost:3100/' },
    { source: '/mcp/:path*', destination: 'http://localhost:3100/:path*' },
  ];
}
```

But that would require the MCP server to run on the same host as Next.js (e.g. same container or same pod). If MCP is a separate container, we'd need the hostname (e.g. `http://checkion-mcp:3100` in Docker network). So the deployment would be: one app that runs both Next.js and MCP? Or two containers and Next.js proxies to the other? In Coolify, typically you have one "Application" with one container. So either:
- Single container: run both Next.js and MCP (e.g. in one Dockerfile with two processes, or a process manager). Then rewrite to localhost:3100.
- Two containers in Coolify: possible if Coolify supports multi-container apps. Then CHECKION container would need to proxy to the MCP container - we'd need the MCP service name in the network.

Actually re-reading the user message: "das mit dem proxy kannst du doch festlegen oder nicht? also per deployment usw." - they're asking if WE can define the proxy (in code/repo) so that deployment exposes MCP under the same domain. So they want a solution in the project that makes MCP reachable at e.g. checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp when deployed.

Best approach that we can "festlegen" in the repo:
1. **Next.js rewrites** in checkion's next.config.js (or next.config.mjs/ts) that proxy `/mcp` and `/mcp/*` to an internal URL. The internal URL could be an env var like `MCP_SERVER_INTERNAL_URL=http://localhost:3100` for same-machine, or `http://checkion-mcp:3100` for Docker Compose with a service named checkion-mcp. So we add the rewrites and document that for "same domain" deployment you need to run MCP alongside (same container or same host) or set the env to the MCP container URL.
2. **Docker Compose** in the repo that runs CHECKION + MCP + nginx/traefik and routes / to CHECKION and /mcp to MCP. That would be a full deployment config.

I'll go with Next.js rewrites because CHECKION is already a Next.js app and we don't know their exact Coolify setup. We can add rewrites that point to an env var; if they run MCP in the same Coolify "stack" with a known service name, they set the env. Next.js rewrites - the destination for external URLs might need to be absolute. Let me check Next.js docs - rewrites() destination can be a path or full URL in recent Next.js. So we can do:

```js
async rewrites() {
  const mcpUrl = process.env.MCP_SERVER_URL; // e.g. http://localhost:3100 or http://checkion-mcp:3100
  if (!mcpUrl) return [];
  const base = mcpUrl.replace(/\/$/, '');
  return [
    { source: '/mcp', destination: `${base}/` },
    { source: '/mcp/:path*', destination: `${base}/:path*` },
  ];
}
```

Der Proxy ist jetzt im Deployment festgelegt. Übersicht:

---

## Was wurde geändert

### 1. **Next.js Rewrites** (`next.config.mjs`)

- Wenn die Umgebungsvariable **`MCP_SERVER_URL`** gesetzt ist, leitet die CHECKION-App alle Anfragen an **`/mcp`** und **`/mcp/*`** an diese URL weiter.
- Beispiele: `http://localhost:3100` (gleicher Host) oder `http://<mcp-service>:3100` (interner Hostname des MCP-Containers).

### 2. **Konstante** (`lib/constants.ts`)

- **`ENV_MCP_SERVER_URL`** = `'MCP_SERVER_URL'` – zentraler Verweis auf die Env-Variable (z. B. für Doku oder Skripte).

### 3. **Dokumentation**

- **`mcp-server/README.md`**: Coolify um „Option B: MCP unter derselben Domain (Proxy)“ ergänzt.
- **`knowledge/checkion-mcp-server.md`**: Abschnitt „Same-domain proxy“ ergänzt.

---

## So erreichst du den MCP unter deiner CHECKION-Domain

1. **Coolify:** Zwei Applications – CHECKION (Haupt-App) und MCP (eigenes Deployment aus `mcp-server/Dockerfile`).
2. **CHECKION-App:** In den **Umgebungsvariablen** setzen:
   - `MCP_SERVER_URL=http://<INTERNER-NAME-DES-MCP-SERVICES>:3100`  
   In Coolify ist das meist der **Application-Name** oder **Service-Name** des MCP-Deployments (z. B. `checkion-mcp` → `http://checkion-mcp:3100`). Den genauen Hostnamen siehst du in Coolify in den Netzwerk-/Service-Details.
3. **MCP-URL für Cursor/Claude:**  
   **`https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/mcp`**

Der Proxy wird damit über die Umgebungsvariable und das Deployment gesteuert; keine weitere Konfiguration im Code nötig.

---

## Anpassung

**Datei:** `MSQDX-DS/msqdx-design-system/packages/figma-plugin/src/code/schema.ts`  

- **Änderung:** `cornerRadius?: number;` im Interface `MsqdxComponent` ergänzt (z. B. nach `strokes`).

Damit sind alle Verwendungen von `cornerRadius` in den Figma-Plugin-Definitionen typkonform.

---

## Deployment

- Wenn **CHECKION** und **MSQDX-DS** getrennte Repos sind: Änderung in MSQDX-DS committen und pushen. Beim CHECKION-Build muss die **aktuelle Version** von MSQDX-DS verwendet werden (z. B. aktualisierter Submodule-Stand oder neues Clone).
- Wenn beides im **gleichen Monorepo** liegt: Einmal committen; der nächste CHECKION-Build nutzt dann die angepasste `schema.ts`.

Wenn der CHECKION-Build das Design-System als **eigenen Clone/Pfad** (z. B. `/msqdx-design-system`) in den Container holt, muss dieser Pfad auf das Repo/Commit zeigen, in dem die Schema-Änderung liegt.

   → `padding: { top: 8, right: 12, bottom: 8, left: 12 }`

Das Schema erlaubt nur `{ top, right, bottom, left }` (bzw. eine Zahl). Die Werte sind unverändert, nur die Form ist typkonform. Nach Commit/Push von MSQDX-DS sollte der CHECKION-Build durchlaufen.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The error says `alignItems: 'END'` is not assignable to type '"MIN" | "CENTER" | "MAX" | "STRETCH" | undefined'. So the schema has alignItems as MIN | CENTER | MAX | STRETCH but Figma (or the code) uses 'END'. In Figma's Auto Layout, the counter axis alignment can be MIN, CENTER, MAX, or STRETCH. In some APIs "END" is used as an alias for "MAX" (end of the axis). So I need to either add 'END' to the schema or change the component to use 'MAX' instead. Adding 'END' to the schema is the minimal fix if the createNode logic maps END to Figma's equivalent; otherwise using 'MAX' in the component is correct since Figma uses MAX not END. Let me check the Figma plugin API - in Figma, primaryAxisAlignItems and counterAxisAlignItems use "MIN", "CENTER", "MAX", "STRETCH". So "END" isn't a valid Figma value; "MAX" is the end. So the fix could be either 1) add 'END' to the schema and in createNode map 'END' -> 'MAX' when setting the property, or 2) change components.ts line 604 from alignItems: 'END' to alignItems: 'MAX'. Option 2 is simpler and semantically correct (MAX = end of axis). I'll do option 2.
</details></think>

**In** `MSQDX-DS/msqdx-design-system/packages/react/src/components/atoms/Avatar/MsqdxAvatar.tsx`:

   `React.ComponentType<MsqdxAvatarProps & { avatarSize: ...; avatarVariant: ...; bordered: boolean; avatarShadow: ... }>`

Ursache ist meist unterschiedliche `@types/react`-Versionen (z. B. React 18 vs. 19) zwischen MUI und der App. Der Cast ist dafür der übliche Workaround.

**In** `MSQDX-DS/msqdx-design-system/packages/react/src/components/atoms/Badge/MsqdxBadge.tsx`:

Die Überprüfung ist durch. Hier die Änderungen:

## Überblick

**1. Styled-Komponenten (React 18/19 JSX-Typen)**  
Alle `styled()`-Komponenten im MSQDX-React-Paket wurden mit `as React.ComponentType<any>` versehen, damit sie zuverlässig als JSX-Komponenten gelten und der bekannte Fehler (*StyledComponent … is not a valid JSX element type / ReactNode | Promise<ReactNode>*) nicht mehr auftritt.

**Betroffene Dateien:**

| Komponente | Styled-Variablen |
|------------|------------------|
| **MsqdxPrismionResult** | `RichContentBox` |
| **MsqdxPrismionPorts** | `PortButton`, `MenuButton` |
| **MsqdxConnectorMenu** | `CenterButton`, `ActionButton` |
| **MsqdxSwitchField** | `StyledSwitch` |
| **MsqdxTabs** | `StyledTabs`, `StyledTab` |
| **MsqdxPrismionToolbar** | `RadialButton` |
| **MsqdxIconButton** | `StyledIconButton` |
| **MsqdxUserBadge** | `StyledWrapper`, `LiveBadge`, `TooltipBox` |
| **MsqdxLogoMark** | `StyledLogoMark` |
| **MsqdxLogo** | `StyledLogo` |
| **MsqdxAccordion** | `SummaryButton`, `SummaryButtonHorizontal`, `ExpandIconButton`, `DetailsVertical`, `DetailsInnerVertical`, `PanelHorizontal`, `DetailsInnerHorizontal` |
| **MsqdxTooltip** | `StyledTooltip` |
| **MsqdxToolbar** | `StyledToolbar` |
| **MsqdxTextareaField** | `StyledInputWrapper`, `StyledTextField` |
| **MsqdxStepper** | `StyledStepper`, `StyledStepIcon` |

**2. TypeScript (implizites `any`)**  
Zusätzlich wurden die vom Design-System-Build gemeldeten impliziten `any`-Stellen typisiert:

- **MsqdxTabs.tsx**: `onChange`-Parameter als `(_: React.SyntheticEvent, newValue: string | number)`.
- **MsqdxConnectorMenu.tsx**, **MsqdxPrismionPorts.tsx**, **MsqdxPrismionToolbar.tsx**: Event-Handler mit `(e: React.MouseEvent<HTMLElement>)`.

**3. React-Import**  
Wo nötig wurde `import React from "react"` ergänzt (z. B. **MsqdxIconButton**, **MsqdxLogoMark**, **MsqdxLogo**), damit `React.ComponentType` und die Event-Typen verfügbar sind.

Der Build des MSQDX-Design-Systems (`npm run build` im Repo `msqdx-design-system`) läuft damit durch.  
Der CHECKION-Build schlägt bei dir weiterhin wegen fehlender Auflösung von `@msqdx/react` und `@msqdx/tokens` fehl – das ist ein Workspace-/Link-Setup (z. B. `npm link` oder Monorepo-Verzeichnisstruktur), nicht die Komponenten selbst.

---

_**User**_

kann es weitere probleme geben die wir jetzt schon fixen können?

[Next.js 16 (beta) | Next.js](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-beta)\n\n# Next.js 16: Middleware Deprecated, Replaced with Proxy\n\nIn Next.js 16, the &#96;middleware&#96; file convention has been deprecated and renamed to &#96;proxy&#96;.[1] This change clarifies the purpose of the feature and improves developer experience.\n\n## Why the Change\n\nThe term \"middleware\" was confusing because it's commonly associated with Express.js middleware, leading to misuse of the feature.[1] The new name \"proxy\" better reflects what the feature does—it acts as a network boundary in front of the app, intercepting and forwarding requests.[1]\n\n## Key Differences\n\nThe main runtime change is significant:[2]\n\n| Aspect | Middleware (Next.js 15) | Proxy (Next.js 16) |\n|--------|------------------------|-------------------|\n| Default runtime | Edge | Node.js |\n| Node.js API access | Limited | Full |\n\nThe proxy runs on Node.js runtime by default, providing full access to Node APIs and better performance for CPU-intensive operations.[2]\n\n## How to Migrate\n\nNext.js provides a codemod to automate migration:[1]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nnpx @next/codemod@canary middleware-to-proxy .\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe migration requires only two manual changes:\n1. Rename &#96;middleware.ts&#96; to &#96;proxy.ts&#96;[2]\n2. Rename the exported function from &#96;middleware()&#96; to &#96;proxy()&#96;[2]\n\nEverything else remains identical—&#96;NextRequest&#96;, &#96;NextResponse&#96;, and the &#96;config&#96; object with matchers work the same way.[2] If you had &#96;export const runtime = 'edge'&#96;, you can remove it since Node.js is now the default.[2]\n\nThe upgrade codemod for Next.js 16 includes middleware-to-proxy migration as one of its features.[4]",
