```json
{
  "markdown": "## Why This Error Occurred\n\nYou are using the &#96;middleware&#96; file convention, which is deprecated and has been renamed to [&#96;proxy&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy).\n\n## Migration to Proxy\n\n### Why the Change\n\nThe reason behind the renaming of &#96;middleware&#96; is that the term \"middleware\" can often be confused with Express.js middleware, leading to a misinterpretation of its purpose. Also, Middleware is highly capable, so it may encourage the usage; however, this feature is recommended to be used as a last resort.\n\nNext.js is moving forward to provide better APIs with better ergonomics so that developers can achieve their goals without Middleware. This is the reason behind the renaming of &#96;middleware&#96;.\n\n### Why \"Proxy\"\n\nThe name Proxy clarifies what Middleware is capable of. The term \"proxy\" implies that it has a network boundary in front of the app, which is the behavior of Middleware. Also, Middleware defaults to run at the [Edge Runtime](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/edge), which can run closer to the client, separated from the app's region. These behaviors align better with the term \"proxy\" and provide a clearer purpose of the feature.\n\n### How to Migrate\n\nWe recommend users avoid relying on Middleware unless no other options exist. Our goal is to give them APIs with better ergonomics so they can achieve their goals without Middleware.\n\nThe term “middleware” often confuses users with Express.js middleware, which can encourage misuse. To clarify our direction, we are renaming the file convention to “proxy.” This highlights that we are moving away from Middleware, breaking down its overloaded features, and making the Proxy clear in its purpose.\n\nNext.js provides a codemod to migrate from &#96;middleware.ts&#96; to &#96;proxy.ts&#96;. You can run the following command to migrate:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nnpx @next/codemod@canary middleware-to-proxy .\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe codemod will rename the file and the function name from &#96;middleware&#96; to &#96;proxy&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;diff\n// middleware.ts -\u003e proxy.ts\n\n- export function middleware() {\n+ export function proxy() {\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Useful Links\n\n- [Proxy Docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy)",
  "url": "https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/middleware-to-proxy"
}
```

```json
{
  "markdown": "\u003e Note: The &#96;middleware&#96; file convention is deprecated and has been renamed to &#96;proxy&#96;. See [Migration to Proxy](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy#migration-to-proxy) for more details.\n\nThe &#96;proxy.js|ts&#96; file is used to write [Proxy](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/proxy) and run code on the server before a request is completed. Then, based on the incoming request, you can modify the response by rewriting, redirecting, modifying the request or response headers, or responding directly.\n\nProxy executes before routes are rendered. It's particularly useful for implementing custom server-side logic like authentication, logging, or handling redirects.\n\n\u003e To pass information from Proxy to your application, use [headers](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy#setting-headers), [cookies](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy#using-cookies), [rewrites](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-response#rewrite), [redirects](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-response#redirect), or the URL.\n\u003e \n\u003e Proxy is meant to be invoked separately of your render code and in optimized cases deployed to your CDN for fast redirect/rewrite handling, you should not attempt relying on shared modules or globals.\n\u003e \n\u003e Good to know:\n\nCreate a &#96;proxy.ts&#96; (or &#96;.js&#96;) file in the project root, or inside &#96;src&#96; if applicable, so that it is located at the same level as &#96;pages&#96; or &#96;app&#96;.\n\nIf you’ve customized [&#96;pageExtensions&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/pageExtensions), for example to &#96;.page.ts&#96; or &#96;.page.js&#96;, name your file &#96;proxy.page.ts&#96; or &#96;proxy.page.js&#96; accordingly.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\nimport { NextResponse, NextRequest } from 'next/server'\n\n// This function can be marked &#96;async&#96; if using &#96;await&#96; inside\nexport function proxy(request: NextRequest) {\n  return NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/home', request.url))\n}\n\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: '/about/:path*',\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nimport { NextResponse } from 'next/server'\n\n// This function can be marked &#96;async&#96; if using &#96;await&#96; inside\nexport function proxy(request) {\n  return NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/home', request.url))\n}\n\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: '/about/:path*',\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Exports\n\n### Proxy function\n\nThe file must export a single function, either as a default export or named &#96;proxy&#96;. Note that multiple proxy from the same file are not supported.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\n// Example of default export\nexport default function proxy(request) {\n  // Proxy logic\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Config object (optional)\n\nOptionally, a config object can be exported alongside the Proxy function. This object includes the [matcher](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy#matcher) to specify paths where the Proxy applies.\n\n### Matcher\n\nThe &#96;matcher&#96; option allows you to target specific paths for the Proxy to run on. You can specify these paths in several ways:\n\n- For multiple paths: Use an array to list multiple paths, such as &#96;matcher: ['/about', '/contact']&#96;, which applies the Proxy to both &#96;/about&#96; and &#96;/contact&#96;.\n- For a single path: Directly use a string to define the path, like &#96;'/about'&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: ['/about/:path*', '/dashboard/:path*'],\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAdditionally, the &#96;matcher&#96; option supports complex path specifications using regular expressions. For example, you can exclude certain paths with a regular expression matcher:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: [\n    // Exclude API routes, static files, image optimizations, and .png files\n    '/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|.*\\\\.png$).*)',\n  ],\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis enables precise control over which paths to include or exclude.\n\nThe &#96;matcher&#96; option accepts an array of objects with the following keys:\n\n- &#96;missing&#96; (optional): Focuses on conditions where certain request elements are absent, like missing headers or cookies.\n- &#96;has&#96; (optional): Specifies conditions based on the presence of specific request elements such as headers, query parameters, or cookies.\n- &#96;locale&#96; (optional): A boolean that, when set to &#96;false&#96;, ignores locale-based routing in path matching.\n- &#96;source&#96;: The path or pattern used to match the request paths. It can be a string for direct path matching or a pattern for more complex matching.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: [\n    {\n      source: '/api/:path*',\n      locale: false,\n      has: [\n        { type: 'header', key: 'Authorization', value: 'Bearer Token' },\n        { type: 'query', key: 'userId', value: '123' },\n      ],\n      missing: [{ type: 'cookie', key: 'session', value: 'active' }],\n    },\n  ],\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe &#96;source&#96; path patterns:\n\n1. Are anchored to the start of the path: &#96;/about&#96; matches &#96;/about&#96; and &#96;/about/team&#96; but not &#96;/blog/about&#96;\n2. Can use regular expression enclosed in parenthesis: &#96;/about/(.*)&#96; is the same as &#96;/about/:path*&#96;\n3. Can have modifiers on named parameters (starting with &#96;:&#96;): &#96;/about/:path*&#96; matches &#96;/about/a/b/c&#96; because &#96;*&#96; is zero or more. &#96;?&#96; is zero or one and &#96;+&#96; one or more\n4. Can include named parameters: &#96;/about/:path&#96; matches &#96;/about/a&#96; and &#96;/about/b&#96; but not &#96;/about/a/c&#96;\n5. MUST start with &#96;/&#96;\n\nRead more details on [path-to-regexp](https://github.com/pillarjs/path-to-regexp#path-to-regexp-1) documentation.\n\n\u003e - For backward compatibility, Next.js always considers &#96;/public&#96; as &#96;/public/index&#96;. Therefore, a matcher of &#96;/public/:path&#96; will match.\n\u003e - The &#96;matcher&#96; values need to be constants so they can be statically analyzed at build-time. Dynamic values such as variables will be ignored.\n\u003e \n\u003e Good to know:\n\n## Params\n\n### &#96;request&#96;\n\nWhen defining Proxy, the default export function accepts a single parameter, &#96;request&#96;. This parameter is an instance of &#96;NextRequest&#96;, which represents the incoming HTTP request.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\nimport type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function proxy(request: NextRequest) {\n  // Proxy logic goes here\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport function proxy(request) {\n  // Proxy logic goes here\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n\u003e - &#96;NextRequest&#96; is a type that represents incoming HTTP requests in Next.js Proxy, whereas [&#96;NextResponse&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy#nextresponse) is a class used to manipulate and send back HTTP responses.\n\u003e \n\u003e Good to know:\n\n## NextResponse\n\nThe &#96;NextResponse&#96; API allows you to:\n\n- Set response headers\n- Set response cookies\n- Set request headers for API Routes, &#96;getServerSideProps&#96;, and &#96;rewrite&#96; destinations\n- &#96;rewrite&#96; the response by displaying a given URL\n- &#96;redirect&#96; the incoming request to a different URL\n\nTo produce a response from Proxy, you can:\n\n1. return a &#96;NextResponse&#96; directly. See [Producing a Response](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy#producing-a-response)\n2. &#96;rewrite&#96; to a route ([Page](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) or [Route Handler](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route)) that produces a response\n\n\u003e Good to know: For redirects, you can also use &#96;Response.redirect&#96; instead of &#96;NextResponse.redirect&#96;.\n\n## Execution order\n\nProxy will be invoked for every route in your project. Given this, it's crucial to use [matchers](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy#matcher) to precisely target or exclude specific routes. The following is the execution order:\n\n1. &#96;fallback&#96; (&#96;rewrites&#96;) from &#96;next.config.js&#96;\n2. Dynamic Routes (&#96;/blog/[slug]&#96;)\n3. &#96;afterFiles&#96; (&#96;rewrites&#96;) from &#96;next.config.js&#96;\n4. Filesystem routes (&#96;public/&#96;, &#96;_next/static/&#96;, &#96;pages/&#96;, &#96;app/&#96;, etc.)\n5. &#96;beforeFiles&#96; (&#96;rewrites&#96;) from &#96;next.config.js&#96;\n6. Proxy (&#96;rewrites&#96;, &#96;redirects&#96;, etc.)\n7. &#96;redirects&#96; from &#96;next.config.js&#96;\n8. &#96;headers&#96; from &#96;next.config.js&#96;\n\n## Runtime\n\nProxy defaults to using the Node.js runtime. The [&#96;runtime&#96;](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#runtime) config option is not available in Proxy files. Setting the &#96;runtime&#96; config option in Proxy will throw an error.\n\n## Advanced Proxy flags\n\nIn &#96;v13.1&#96; of Next.js two additional flags were introduced for proxy, &#96;skipMiddlewareUrlNormalize&#96; and &#96;skipTrailingSlashRedirect&#96; to handle advanced use cases.\n\n&#96;skipTrailingSlashRedirect&#96; disables Next.js redirects for adding or removing trailing slashes. This allows custom handling inside proxy to maintain the trailing slash for some paths but not others, which can make incremental migrations easier.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nmodule.exports = {\n  skipTrailingSlashRedirect: true,\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nconst legacyPrefixes = ['/docs', '/blog']\n\nexport default async function proxy(req) {\n  const { pathname } = req.nextUrl\n\n  if (legacyPrefixes.some((prefix) =\u003e pathname.startsWith(prefix))) {\n    return NextResponse.next()\n  }\n\n  // apply trailing slash handling\n  if (\n    !pathname.endsWith('/') \u0026\u0026\n    !pathname.match(/((?!\\.well-known(?:\\/.*)?)(?:[^/]+\\/)*[^/]+\\.\\w+)/)\n  ) {\n    return NextResponse.redirect(\n      new URL(&#96;${req.nextUrl.pathname}/&#96;, req.nextUrl)\n    )\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;skipMiddlewareUrlNormalize&#96; allows for disabling the URL normalization in Next.js to make handling direct visits and client-transitions the same. In some advanced cases, this option provides full control by using the original URL.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nmodule.exports = {\n  skipMiddlewareUrlNormalize: true,\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport default async function proxy(req) {\n  const { pathname } = req.nextUrl\n\n  // GET /_next/data/build-id/hello.json\n\n  console.log(pathname)\n  // with the flag this now /_next/data/build-id/hello.json\n  // without the flag this would be normalized to /hello\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Examples\n\n### Conditional Statements\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;ts\nimport { NextResponse } from 'next/server'\nimport type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function proxy(request: NextRequest) {\n  if (request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith('/about')) {\n    return NextResponse.rewrite(new URL('/about-2', request.url))\n  }\n\n  if (request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith('/dashboard')) {\n    return NextResponse.rewrite(new URL('/dashboard/user', request.url))\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nimport { NextResponse } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function proxy(request) {\n  if (request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith('/about')) {\n    return NextResponse.rewrite(new URL('/about-2', request.url))\n  }\n\n  if (request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith('/dashboard')) {\n    return NextResponse.rewrite(new URL('/dashboard/user', request.url))\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Using Cookies\n\nCookies are regular headers. On a &#96;Request&#96;, they are stored in the &#96;Cookie&#96; header. On a &#96;Response&#96; they are in the &#96;Set-Cookie&#96; header. Next.js provides a convenient way to access and manipulate these cookies through the &#96;cookies&#96; extension on &#96;NextRequest&#96; and &#96;NextResponse&#96;.\n\n1. For outgoing responses, &#96;cookies&#96; have the following methods &#96;get&#96;, &#96;getAll&#96;, &#96;set&#96;, and &#96;delete&#96;.\n2. For incoming requests, &#96;cookies&#96; comes with the following methods: &#96;get&#96;, &#96;getAll&#96;, &#96;set&#96;, and &#96;delete&#96; cookies. You can check for the existence of a cookie with &#96;has&#96; or remove all cookies with &#96;clear&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;ts\nimport { NextResponse } from 'next/server'\nimport type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function proxy(request: NextRequest) {\n  // Assume a \"Cookie:nextjs=fast\" header to be present on the incoming request\n  // Getting cookies from the request using the &#96;RequestCookies&#96; API\n  let cookie = request.cookies.get('nextjs')\n  console.log(cookie) // =\u003e { name: 'nextjs', value: 'fast', Path: '/' }\n  const allCookies = request.cookies.getAll()\n  console.log(allCookies) // =\u003e [{ name: 'nextjs', value: 'fast' }]\n\n  request.cookies.has('nextjs') // =\u003e true\n  request.cookies.delete('nextjs')\n  request.cookies.has('nextjs') // =\u003e false\n\n  // Setting cookies on the response using the &#96;ResponseCookies&#96; API\n  const response = NextResponse.next()\n  response.cookies.set('vercel', 'fast')\n  response.cookies.set({\n    name: 'vercel',\n    value: 'fast',\n    path: '/',\n  })\n  cookie = response.cookies.get('vercel')\n  console.log(cookie) // =\u003e { name: 'vercel', value: 'fast', Path: '/' }\n  // The outgoing response will have a &#96;Set-Cookie:vercel=fast;path=/&#96; header.\n\n  return response\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nimport { NextResponse } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function proxy(request) {\n  // Assume a \"Cookie:nextjs=fast\" header to be present on the incoming request\n  // Getting cookies from the request using the &#96;RequestCookies&#96; API\n  let cookie = request.cookies.get('nextjs')\n  console.log(cookie) // =\u003e { name: 'nextjs', value: 'fast', Path: '/' }\n  const allCookies = request.cookies.getAll()\n  console.log(allCookies) // =\u003e [{ name: 'nextjs', value: 'fast' }]\n\n  request.cookies.has('nextjs') // =\u003e true\n  request.cookies.delete('nextjs')\n  request.cookies.has('nextjs') // =\u003e false\n\n  // Setting cookies on the response using the &#96;ResponseCookies&#96; API\n  const response = NextResponse.next()\n  response.cookies.set('vercel', 'fast')\n  response.cookies.set({\n    name: 'vercel',\n    value: 'fast',\n    path: '/',\n  })\n  cookie = response.cookies.get('vercel')\n  console.log(cookie) // =\u003e { name: 'vercel', value: 'fast', Path: '/' }\n  // The outgoing response will have a &#96;Set-Cookie:vercel=fast;path=/&#96; header.\n\n  return response\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Setting Headers\n\nYou can set request and response headers using the &#96;NextResponse&#96; API (setting request headers is available since Next.js v13.0.0).\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;ts\nimport { NextResponse } from 'next/server'\nimport type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function proxy(request: NextRequest) {\n  // Clone the request headers and set a new header &#96;x-hello-from-proxy1&#96;\n  const requestHeaders = new Headers(request.headers)\n  requestHeaders.set('x-hello-from-proxy1', 'hello')\n\n  // You can also set request headers in NextResponse.next\n  const response = NextResponse.next({\n    request: {\n      // New request headers\n      headers: requestHeaders,\n    },\n  })\n\n  // Set a new response header &#96;x-hello-from-proxy2&#96;\n  response.headers.set('x-hello-from-proxy2', 'hello')\n  return response\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nimport { NextResponse } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function proxy(request) {\n  // Clone the request headers and set a new header &#96;x-hello-from-proxy1&#96;\n  const requestHeaders = new Headers(request.headers)\n  requestHeaders.set('x-hello-from-proxy1', 'hello')\n\n  // You can also set request headers in NextResponse.next\n  const response = NextResponse.next({\n    request: {\n      // New request headers\n      headers: requestHeaders,\n    },\n  })\n\n  // Set a new response header &#96;x-hello-from-proxy2&#96;\n  response.headers.set('x-hello-from-proxy2', 'hello')\n  return response\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nNote that the snippet uses:\n\n- NOT &#96;NextResponse.next({ headers: requestHeaders })&#96; which makes &#96;requestHeaders&#96; available to clients\n- &#96;NextResponse.next({ request: { headers: requestHeaders } })&#96; to make &#96;requestHeaders&#96; available upstream\n\nLearn more in [NextResponse headers in Proxy](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-response#next).\n\n\u003e Good to know: Avoid setting large headers as it might cause [431 Request Header Fields Too Large](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/431) error depending on your backend web server configuration.\n\n### CORS\n\nYou can set CORS headers in Proxy to allow cross-origin requests, including [simple](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS#simple_requests) and [preflighted](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS#preflighted_requests) requests.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\nimport { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server'\n\nconst allowedOrigins = ['https://acme.com', 'https://my-app.org']\n\nconst corsOptions = {\n  'Access-Control-Allow-Methods': 'GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS',\n  'Access-Control-Allow-Headers': 'Content-Type, Authorization',\n}\n\nexport function proxy(request: NextRequest) {\n  // Check the origin from the request\n  const origin = request.headers.get('origin') ?? ''\n  const isAllowedOrigin = allowedOrigins.includes(origin)\n\n  // Handle preflighted requests\n  const isPreflight = request.method === 'OPTIONS'\n\n  if (isPreflight) {\n    const preflightHeaders = {\n      ...(isAllowedOrigin \u0026\u0026 { 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin': origin }),\n      ...corsOptions,\n    }\n    return NextResponse.json({}, { headers: preflightHeaders })\n  }\n\n  // Handle simple requests\n  const response = NextResponse.next()\n\n  if (isAllowedOrigin) {\n    response.headers.set('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', origin)\n  }\n\n  Object.entries(corsOptions).forEach(([key, value]) =\u003e {\n    response.headers.set(key, value)\n  })\n\n  return response\n}\n\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: '/api/:path*',\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\nimport { NextResponse } from 'next/server'\n\nconst allowedOrigins = ['https://acme.com', 'https://my-app.org']\n\nconst corsOptions = {\n  'Access-Control-Allow-Methods': 'GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS',\n  'Access-Control-Allow-Headers': 'Content-Type, Authorization',\n}\n\nexport function proxy(request) {\n  // Check the origin from the request\n  const origin = request.headers.get('origin') ?? ''\n  const isAllowedOrigin = allowedOrigins.includes(origin)\n\n  // Handle preflighted requests\n  const isPreflight = request.method === 'OPTIONS'\n\n  if (isPreflight) {\n    const preflightHeaders = {\n      ...(isAllowedOrigin \u0026\u0026 { 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin': origin }),\n      ...corsOptions,\n    }\n    return NextResponse.json({}, { headers: preflightHeaders })\n  }\n\n  // Handle simple requests\n  const response = NextResponse.next()\n\n  if (isAllowedOrigin) {\n    response.headers.set('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', origin)\n  }\n\n  Object.entries(corsOptions).forEach(([key, value]) =\u003e {\n    response.headers.set(key, value)\n  })\n\n  return response\n}\n\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: '/api/:path*',\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n\u003e Good to know: You can configure CORS headers for individual routes in [Route Handlers](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route#cors).\n\n### Producing a response\n\nYou can respond from Proxy directly by returning a &#96;Response&#96; or &#96;NextResponse&#96; instance. (This is available since [Next.js v13.1.0](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-13-1#nextjs-advanced-proxy))\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;ts\nimport type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'\nimport { isAuthenticated } from '@lib/auth'\n\n// Limit the proxy to paths starting with &#96;/api/&#96;\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: '/api/:function*',\n}\n\nexport function proxy(request: NextRequest) {\n  // Call our authentication function to check the request\n  if (!isAuthenticated(request)) {\n    // Respond with JSON indicating an error message\n    return Response.json(\n      { success: false, message: 'authentication failed' },\n      { status: 401 }\n    )\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nimport { isAuthenticated } from '@lib/auth'\n\n// Limit the proxy to paths starting with &#96;/api/&#96;\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: '/api/:function*',\n}\n\nexport function proxy(request) {\n  // Call our authentication function to check the request\n  if (!isAuthenticated(request)) {\n    // Respond with JSON indicating an error message\n    return Response.json(\n      { success: false, message: 'authentication failed' },\n      { status: 401 }\n    )\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Negative matching\n\nThe &#96;matcher&#96; config allows full regex so matching like negative lookaheads or character matching is supported. An example of a negative lookahead to match all except specific paths can be seen here:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: [\n    /*\n     * Match all request paths except for the ones starting with:\n     * - api (API routes)\n     * - _next/static (static files)\n     * - _next/image (image optimization files)\n     * - favicon.ico, sitemap.xml, robots.txt (metadata files)\n     */\n    '/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico|sitemap.xml|robots.txt).*)',\n  ],\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nYou can also bypass Proxy for certain requests by using the &#96;missing&#96; or &#96;has&#96; arrays, or a combination of both:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport const config = {\n  matcher: [\n    /*\n     * Match all request paths except for the ones starting with:\n     * - api (API routes)\n     * - _next/static (static files)\n     * - _next/image (image optimization files)\n     * - favicon.ico, sitemap.xml, robots.txt (metadata files)\n     */\n    {\n      source:\n        '/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico|sitemap.xml|robots.txt).*)',\n      missing: [\n        { type: 'header', key: 'next-router-prefetch' },\n        { type: 'header', key: 'purpose', value: 'prefetch' },\n      ],\n    },\n\n    {\n      source:\n        '/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico|sitemap.xml|robots.txt).*)',\n      has: [\n        { type: 'header', key: 'next-router-prefetch' },\n        { type: 'header', key: 'purpose', value: 'prefetch' },\n      ],\n    },\n\n    {\n      source:\n        '/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico|sitemap.xml|robots.txt).*)',\n      has: [{ type: 'header', key: 'x-present' }],\n      missing: [{ type: 'header', key: 'x-missing', value: 'prefetch' }],\n    },\n  ],\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n\u003e Even when &#96;_next/data&#96; is excluded in a negative matcher pattern, proxy will still be invoked for &#96;_next/data&#96; routes. This is intentional behavior to prevent accidental security issues where you might protect a page but forget to protect the corresponding data route.\n\u003e \n\u003e Good to know:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nexport const config = {\n  matcher:\n    '/((?!api|_next/data|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico|sitemap.xml|robots.txt).*)',\n}\n\n// Proxy will still run for /_next/data/* routes despite being excluded\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### &#96;waitUntil&#96; and &#96;NextFetchEvent&#96;\n\nThe &#96;NextFetchEvent&#96; object extends the native [&#96;FetchEvent&#96;](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/FetchEvent) object, and includes the [&#96;waitUntil()&#96;](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/ExtendableEvent/waitUntil) method.\n\nThe &#96;waitUntil()&#96; method takes a promise as an argument, and extends the lifetime of the Proxy until the promise settles. This is useful for performing work in the background.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;ts\nimport { NextResponse } from 'next/server'\nimport type { NextFetchEvent, NextRequest } from 'next/server'\n\nexport function proxy(req: NextRequest, event: NextFetchEvent) {\n  event.waitUntil(\n    fetch('https://my-analytics-platform.com', {\n      method: 'POST',\n      body: JSON.stringify({ pathname: req.nextUrl.pathname }),\n    })\n  )\n\n  return NextResponse.next()\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Unit testing (experimental)\n\nStarting in Next.js 15.1, the &#96;next/experimental/testing/server&#96; package contains utilities to help unit test proxy files. Unit testing proxy can help ensure that it's only run on desired paths and that custom routing logic works as intended before code reaches production.\n\nThe &#96;unstable_doesProxyMatch&#96; function can be used to assert whether proxy will run for the provided URL, headers, and cookies.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nimport { unstable_doesProxyMatch } from 'next/experimental/testing/server'\n\nexpect(\n  unstable_doesProxyMatch({\n    config,\n    nextConfig,\n    url: '/test',\n  })\n).toEqual(false)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe entire proxy function can also be tested.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;js\nimport { isRewrite, getRewrittenUrl } from 'next/experimental/testing/server'\n\nconst request = new NextRequest('https://nextjs.org/docs')\nconst response = await proxy(request)\nexpect(isRewrite(response)).toEqual(true)\nexpect(getRewrittenUrl(response)).toEqual('https://other-domain.com/docs')\n// getRedirectUrl could also be used if the response were a redirect\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Platform support\n\n| Deployment Option | Supported |\n| --- | --- |\n| [Node.js server](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#nodejs-server) | Yes |\n| [Docker container](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#docker) | Yes |\n| [Static export](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#static-export) | No |\n| [Adapters](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#adapters) | Platform-specific |\n\nLearn how to [configure Proxy](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/self-hosting#proxy) when self-hosting Next.js.\n\n## Migration to Proxy\n\n### Why the Change\n\nThe reason behind the renaming of &#96;middleware&#96; is that the term \"middleware\" can often be confused with Express.js middleware, leading to a misinterpretation of its purpose. Also, Middleware is highly capable, so it may encourage the usage; however, this feature is recommended to be used as a last resort.\n\nNext.js is moving forward to provide better APIs with better ergonomics so that developers can achieve their goals without Middleware. This is the reason behind the renaming of &#96;middleware&#96;.\n\n### Why \"Proxy\"\n\nThe name Proxy clarifies what Middleware is capable of. The term \"proxy\" implies that it has a network boundary in front of the app, which is the behavior of Middleware. Also, Middleware defaults to run at the [Edge Runtime](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/edge), which can run closer to the client, separated from the app's region. These behaviors align better with the term \"proxy\" and provide a clearer purpose of the feature.\n\n### How to Migrate\n\nWe recommend users avoid relying on Middleware unless no other options exist. Our goal is to give them APIs with better ergonomics so they can achieve their goals without Middleware.\n\nThe term “middleware” often confuses users with Express.js middleware, which can encourage misuse. To clarify our direction, we are renaming the file convention to “proxy.” This highlights that we are moving away from Middleware, breaking down its overloaded features, and making the Proxy clear in its purpose.\n\nNext.js provides a codemod to migrate from &#96;middleware.ts&#96; to &#96;proxy.ts&#96;. You can run the following command to migrate:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nnpx @next/codemod@canary middleware-to-proxy .\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe codemod will rename the file and the function name from &#96;middleware&#96; to &#96;proxy&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;diff\n// middleware.ts -\u003e proxy.ts\n\n- export function middleware() {\n+ export function proxy() {\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Version history\n\n| Version | Changes |\n| --- | --- |\n| &#96;v16.0.0&#96; | Middleware is deprecated and renamed to Proxy |\n| &#96;v15.5.0&#96; | Middleware can now use the Node.js runtime (stable) |\n| &#96;v15.2.0&#96; | Middleware can now use the Node.js runtime (experimental) |\n| &#96;v13.1.0&#96; | Advanced Middleware flags added |\n| &#96;v13.0.0&#96; | Middleware can modify request headers, response headers, and send responses |\n| &#96;v12.2.0&#96; | Middleware is stable, please see the [upgrade guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/middleware-upgrade-guide) |\n| &#96;v12.0.9&#96; | Enforce absolute URLs in Edge Runtime ([PR](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/33410)) |\n| &#96;v12.0.0&#96; | Middleware (Beta) added |\n\n## Learn more about Proxy- [NextRequest](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request)\n\n- API Reference for NextResponse.\n- [NextResponse](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-response)\n- API Reference for NextRequest.\n\n---\n\nFor a semantic overview of all documentation, see [/docs/sitemap.md](https://nextjs.org/docs/sitemap.md)\n\nFor an index of all available documentation, see [/docs/llms.txt](https://nextjs.org/docs/llms.txt)",
  "url": "https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy"
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The proxy uses protectedPaths = [PATH_SCAN, PATH_RESULTS, PATH_DOMAIN, PATH_SETTINGS]. So /projects, /history, /geo-eeat, /journey-agent, /developers are not in that list. The logic is: if path is in protectedPaths OR path === '/', then require session. So /projects would currently NOT be protected - only PATH_SCAN, PATH_RESULTS, PATH_DOMAIN, PATH_SETTINGS and / are protected. If the user wants /projects to be protected they'd need to add PATH_PROJECTS to protectedPaths - that's a product decision. I won't change that unless the projects feature doc says so. The knowledge doc is done. Summary for user is complete.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


Hier die vorbeugenden Anpassungen und die Dokumentation:

---

## 1. **Next.js 16: Middleware → Proxy**

- **middleware.ts** wurde in **proxy.ts** umbenannt.
- Die exportierte Funktion heißt jetzt **`proxy`** statt `middleware`.
- Inhalt und Logik (geschützte Pfade, Auth-Check, Redirects) sind unverändert.
- Die Deprecation-Warnung im Build sollte damit weg sein, sobald du wieder mit verlinktem Design-System baust.

---

## 2. **Wissensdokument**

In **`knowledge/checkion-preventive-fixes.md`** steht:

- **Bereits behoben:** Styled-Komponenten-Typen im Design System, implizite `any` in Events, sichere Route-Params, Middleware→Proxy.
- **Zu beachten:** Design-System-Build/Link, Stories aus dem TS-Build ausgenommen, Zod in `node_modules` nicht anpassen.
- **Kurz:** Nützliche Befehle und Hinweise für künftige Änderungen.

---

## 3. **Weitere mögliche Themen (derzeit nicht geändert)**

| Thema | Status |
|--------|--------|
| **Route-Params** | Bereits überall sicher behandelt (`params.id` etc.). |
| **`@ts-ignore` / `as any`** | Nur in `mcp-server/node_modules/zod` (Drittanbieter), nicht im App-Code. |
| **Projekte schützen** | `/projects` ist aktuell nicht in `protectedPaths`. Sollen Projekte nur für eingeloggte User sichtbar sein, müsste `PATH_PROJECTS` in `proxy.ts` zu `protectedPaths` hinzugefügt werden. |
| **Design-System Stories** | Enthalten teils untypisierte `(e)`-Handler; sind vom React-Paket-Build ausgeschlossen, verursachen also keine Build-Fehler. |

Wenn du willst, können wir als Nächstes z.B. `PATH_PROJECTS` in die geschützten Pfade aufnehmen oder die Proxy-Logik für weitere Routen anpassen.

---

_**Agent (default)**_


Hier die Änderungen:

---

## 1. **MsqdxButton – StyledButton Typ-Fix**

Im Design-System wurde **MsqdxButton.tsx** angepasst:

- **Datei:** `msqdx-design-system/packages/react/src/components/atoms/Button/MsqdxButton.tsx`
- `import React from "react"` ergänzt.
- Nach dem `styled(Button, ...)(...)`-Block die Assertion ergänzt:  
  **`}) as React.ComponentType<any>;`**

Damit gilt `StyledButton` wieder als gültige JSX-Komponente und der Fehler  
`'StyledButton' cannot be used as a JSX component` (ReactNode | Promise<ReactNode> / bigint) tritt nicht mehr auf.

---

## 2. **Docker-Build: Design-System aus GitHub**

Im Docker-Build wird das Design-System so geholt:

```text
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/chbrdk/msqdx-design-system.git /msqdx-design-system
```

Damit der nächste Docker-Build durchläuft, musst du die Änderung an **MsqdxButton** im Repo **chbrdk/msqdx-design-system** committen und pushen.  
Wenn du das Design-System lokal unter z. B. `../MSQDX-DS/msqdx-design-system` pflegst, von dort pushen; die Anpassung ist dort bereits gemacht.

---

## 3. **Hinweis zur Middleware-Warnung**

Die Meldung *"The 'middleware' file convention is deprecated. Please use 'proxy' instead"* erscheint, wenn Next.js noch eine **middleware**-Datei erkennt. In deinem CHECKION-1-Workspace gibt es nur noch **proxy.ts** und keine **middleware.ts**.  

Wenn die Warnung im Docker-Build weiter auftaucht, prüfen:

- ob im **CHECKION-1**-Image/Context wirklich **proxy.ts** verwendet wird und **middleware.ts** nicht mehr existiert bzw. nicht mehr ins Image kopiert wird.

---

_**Agent (default)**_


Ja – der gleiche Fehler kann bei **jeder** Komponente auftreten, die MUI `styled()` nutzt und das Ergebnis als JSX verwendet. Ich habe alle betroffenen Stellen im Design-System angepasst.

---

## Was ich gemacht habe

**Typ-Assertion `as React.ComponentType<any>`** wurde bei allen Styled-Komponenten ergänzt, die sie noch nicht hatten:

| Komponente | Styled-Variablen |
|------------|------------------|
| **MsqdxSnackbar** | StyledSnackbar |
| **MsqdxSlider** | StyledSlider |
| **MsqdxSelect** | StyledFormControl, StyledSelect |
| **MsqdxFormField** | StyledInputWrapper, StyledTextField |
| **MsqdxDialog** | StyledDialog, StyledDialogTitle, StyledDialogContent, StyledDialogActions |
| **MsqdxGlassCard** | StyledCard |
| **MsqdxCheckboxField** | StyledFormControl, StyledCheckbox, StyledFormControlLabel |
| **MsqdxTypography** | StyledTypography |
| **MsqdxRadioField** | StyledFormControl, StyledRadio, StyledFormControlLabel |
| **MsqdxPopover** | StyledPopover |
| **MsqdxCircleContextMenu** | StyledPopover |
| **MsqdxScrollbar** | ScrollbarContainer |

Zusätzlich wurde **MsqdxButton** (bereits vorher behoben) und an den nötigen Stellen **`import React from "react"`** ergänzt (z. B. Select, GlassCard).

---

## Warum das passiert

Unter **React 18/19** und strengen TypeScript-Einstellungen liefert MUI `styled()` einen Typ, bei dem der Rückgabetyp u. a. `ReactNode | Promise<ReactNode>` (inkl. `bigint`) sein kann. Das kollidiert mit der Erwartung „gültige JSX-Komponente“. Die Assertion `as React.ComponentType<any>` sagt TypeScript explizit: „Behandle das als gültige Komponente.“

---

## Build-Status

`npm run build` im MSQDX-Design-System läuft durch. Damit sollten beim nächsten Docker-Build (mit aktuellem Stand von `chbrdk/msqdx-design-system`) keine weiteren „cannot be used as a JSX component“-Fehler durch Styled-Komponenten auftreten.

Die Knowledge-Datei `knowledge/checkion-preventive-fixes.md` ist um die vollständige Liste der angepassten Komponenten ergänzt.

---

---

## Änderungen

1. **`atoms/Card/MsqdxCard.tsx`** (Auslöser des Fehlers)  
   - `import React from "react"` ergänzt  
   - `StyledCard` mit `as React.ComponentType<any>` versehen  

2. **Weitere Atome (vorbeugend)**  
   - **MsqdxProgress**: `ProgressContainer`, `StyledLinearProgress`  
   - **MsqdxLabel**: `StyledLabel`  
   - **MsqdxInput**: `InputWrapper`  
   - **MsqdxDivider**: `StyledDividerRoot`  
   - **MsqdxChip**: `StyledChip`  

Der Build des Design-Systems (`npm run build`) läuft durch.

**Hinweis:** Der Docker-Build nutzt das Repo `chbrdk/msqdx-design-system` per `git clone`. Damit der nächste Deployment-Build funktioniert, müssen diese Änderungen im Design-System-Repo committet und gepusht werden (bzw. der Stand, aus dem gebaut wird, muss diese Anpassungen enthalten).

The build is failing because Next.js is type-checking the design system's Storybook files (e.g. `MsqdxIcon.stories.tsx`). The error says that a Story export has `{ render: () => JSX.Element }` but StoryAnnotations requires `args` to be present.

The cleanest fix so that CHECKION can deploy is to **exclude the design system's `*.stories.tsx` (and possibly `*.stories.ts`) files from the Next.js/TypeScript build**. The CHECKION app doesn't need to compile Storybook files from the linked design system.

Options:
1. In CHECKION's tsconfig.json, add an exclude for `**/*.stories.tsx` and `**/*.stories.ts` under the msqdx-design-system path (or globally for node_modules/transitive deps - but the design system is linked via `ln -snf`, so it's at `./msqdx-design-system`).
2. In CHECKION's next.config, use something to exclude the design system stories from being type-checked (Next.js doesn't have a direct "exclude from typecheck" for linked packages - the typecheck runs over the whole project including transpilePackages and any copied/linked code).
3. Fix the Story file in the design system to satisfy the type (add `args: {}` or proper Story type).

The CHECKION tsconfig already has:
```json
"exclude": ["node_modules", "msqdx-design-system/packages/figma-plugin/**"]
```

So we could add:
- `"**/*.stories.tsx"`
- `"**/*.stories.ts"`
- or more specifically `"msqdx-design-system/**/*.stories.tsx"` and `"msqdx-design-system/**/*.stories.ts"`

That way when Next.js runs "Running TypeScript", it won't type-check the story files. Let me check - does Next.js use the project's tsconfig for that? Yes, typically `next build` runs the TypeScript compiler with the project tsconfig and includes all files in the project. If we exclude `**/*.stories.tsx` and `**/*.stories.ts`, then any stories in the app would also be excluded - but CHECKION might not have any. And the design system stories are under `msqdx-design-system/`. So the safest is to exclude only the design system's stories: `msqdx-design-system/**/*.stories.tsx`, `msqdx-design-system/**/*.stories.ts`.

I'll add that to the CHECKION tsconfig exclude array so the build passes. I'll do this in the CHECKION-1 repo.

Zusammenfassung der Änderungen:

---

## 1. **CHECKION: Story-Dateien vom TypeScript-Build ausnehmen** (`tsconfig.json`)

Damit der Next-Build (und damit das Deployment) nicht an Storybook-Dateien des Design-Systems scheitert, werden alle Story-Dateien vom TypeScript-Check ausgeschlossen:

- `**/*.stories.tsx`
- `**/*.stories.ts`

Diese Dateien werden nicht mehr vom Next.js TypeScript-Lauf geprüft. Sie sind nur für Storybook relevant, nicht für die CHECKION-App.

---

## 2. **Design-System: Icon-Stories typ-konform gemacht** (`MsqdxIcon.stories.tsx`)

In den Stories, die nur `render` und keine `args` hatten, wurden die von Storybook erwarteten `args` ergänzt:

- **Sizes:** `args: { name: 'Settings' }`
- **Weights:** `args: { name: 'Add' }`
- **TokenNames:** `args: { name: 'Add' }`

Damit erfüllen die Stories den erwarteten Typ auch im Design-System-Repo.

---

## Nächste Schritte für dich

1. **CHECKION-1** (wo das Deployment herkommt): Änderung in `tsconfig.json` committen und pushen – danach sollte das Deployment durchlaufen.
2. **Design-System** (z. B. `chbrdk/msqdx-design-system`): Änderungen in `MsqdxIcon.stories.tsx` committen und pushen, damit beim nächsten Docker-Build (der das Repo klont) keine Story-Typfehler mehr auftreten.

Der zentrale Fix für das Deployment ist der **`tsconfig.json`-Exclude** in CHECKION-1. Sobald der gepusht ist, sollte CHECKION wieder deployen.
